Audit mocks/shims, bundled CJS smoke, tsconfig deviation, and possible over-implementation
Run `bun run green-verify` and assemble structured verifier report with findings and coverage verdicts

1	# Story 0: Foundation
     2	
     3	### Summary
     4	<!-- Jira: Summary field -->
     5	Establish canonical spec-pack discovery, tech-design shape detection, durable log initialization, and public prompt-insert detection for `ls-claude-impl`.
     6	
     7	### Description
     8	<!-- Jira: Description field -->
     9	
    10	**User Profile**
    11	
    12	- **Primary User:** A Claude Code orchestration agent operating on behalf of a senior engineer or tech lead
    13	- **Context:** Running a completed spec pack through a disciplined implementation workflow without relying on conversation memory or ad hoc orchestration
    14	- **Mental Model:** "I load a finished spec pack, confirm the environment and options, launch the right workflow through the CLI, and supervise implementation and verification until the epic is complete."
    15	- **Key Constraint:** Claude Code is always available. GPT model lanes depend on whether Codex CLI or Copilot CLI is available in the local environment.
    16	
    17	**Objective**
    18	
    19	Create the behavior-carrying foundation for the runtime and skill surface so the orchestrator can recognize valid spec packs, identify the active tech-design shape, initialize or resume the durable log, and detect optional prompt-insert files before any provider-backed work begins.
    20	
    21	**In Scope**
    22	
    23	- Canonical spec-pack layout recognition for v1
    24	- Two-file versus four-file tech-design shape detection
    25	- `team-impl-log.md` initialization and resume behavior
    26	- Detection of public implementor and verifier prompt-insert files
    27	
    28	**Out of Scope**
    29	
    30	- Verification-gate discovery
    31	- Provider/harness/model default resolution
    32	- Prompt assembly
    33	- Story implementation, verification, or fix routing
    34	
    35	**Dependencies**
    36	
    37	- None
    38	- Uses the completed epic, tech design, and test plan as the source of truth for spec-pack expectations
    39	
    40	### Acceptance Criteria
    41	<!-- Jira: Acceptance Criteria field -->
    42	
    43	**AC-1.1:** The skill recognizes only valid v1 spec-pack layouts
    44	
    45	- **TC-1.1a: Two-file tech design configuration**
    46	  - Given: The epic folder contains `epic.md`, `tech-design.md`, `test-plan.md`, and `stories/`
    47	  - When: The orchestrator initializes a run
    48	  - Then: The skill accepts the spec pack as valid
    49	- **TC-1.1b: Four-file tech design configuration**
    50	  - Given: The epic folder contains `epic.md`, `tech-design.md`, two tech-design companion files, `test-plan.md`, and `stories/`
    51	  - When: The orchestrator initializes a run
    52	  - Then: The skill accepts the spec pack as valid
    53	- **TC-1.1c: Invalid story layout**
    54	  - Given: The epic folder contains `stories.md` instead of `stories/`
    55	  - When: The orchestrator initializes a run
    56	  - Then: The skill reports the layout as invalid for v1 and does not start implementation
    57	- **TC-1.1d: Missing required artifact**
    58	  - Given: A required file or directory is missing
    59	  - When: The orchestrator initializes a run
    60	  - Then: The skill identifies the missing artifact explicitly and does not start implementation
    61	
    62	**AC-1.2:** The skill identifies the tech design configuration before role dispatch begins
    63	
    64	- **TC-1.2a: Two-file configuration identified**
    65	  - Given: Only `tech-design.md` and `test-plan.md` are present
    66	  - When: The skill inspects the epic folder
    67	  - Then: It records the two-file configuration and does not expect companion tech-design files
    68	- **TC-1.2b: Four-file configuration identified**
    69	  - Given: `tech-design.md`, two companion tech-design files, and `test-plan.md` are present
    70	  - When: The skill inspects the epic folder
    71	  - Then: It records the four-file configuration and makes those companions available to relevant role prompts
    72	
    73	**AC-1.3:** The skill initializes `team-impl-log.md` as the durable orchestration record
    74	
    75	- **TC-1.3a: New run**
    76	  - Given: No `team-impl-log.md` exists
    77	  - When: The orchestrator starts a new run
    78	  - Then: The skill creates `team-impl-log.md` and records initial run setup information
    79	- **TC-1.3b: Existing run**
    80	  - Given: `team-impl-log.md` already exists
    81	  - When: The orchestrator starts the skill in the same epic folder
    82	  - Then: The skill reads the existing log and resumes from recorded state rather than starting from scratch
    83	
    84	**AC-1.4:** The skill detects optional public prompt insert files without making them required
    85	
    86	- **TC-1.4a: Both inserts present**
    87	  - Given: `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` and `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` exist in the epic folder
    88	  - When: The skill initializes the run
    89	  - Then: The skill records that both public inserts are available for prompt assembly
    90	- **TC-1.4b: Inserts absent**
    91	  - Given: Neither custom insert file exists
    92	  - When: The skill initializes the run
    93	  - Then: The skill continues normally and records that no custom inserts are active
    94	
    95	### Technical Design
    96	<!-- Jira: Technical Notes or sub-section of Description -->
    97	
    98	**Relevant Data Contracts**
    99	
   100	#### Canonical Epic Folder Contract
   101	
   102	| Path | Required | Description |
   103	|------|----------|-------------|
   104	| `epic.md` | yes | The epic being implemented |
   105	| `stories/` | yes | Directory of individual story files |
   106	| `team-impl-log.md` | created by run | Durable orchestration log |
   107	| `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` | no | Optional public implementor insert |
   108	| `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` | no | Optional public verifier insert |
   109	
   110	#### Valid Tech Design Configurations
   111	
   112	| Configuration | Required Files |
   113	|---------------|----------------|
   114	| Two-file tech design | `tech-design.md`, `test-plan.md` |
   115	| Four-file tech design | `tech-design.md`, two tech-design companion files, `test-plan.md` |
   116	
   117	#### Foundation Command Skeletons
   118	
   119	| Command | Purpose |
   120	|---------|---------|
   121	| `inspect` | Resolve spec-pack layout, story inventory, and insert presence |
   122	| `preflight` | Validate authored run config, gate inputs, provider availability, and prompt assets |
   123	| `story-implement` | Start retained implementor execution for a story |
   124	| `story-verify` | Launch a fresh verifier batch |
   125	| `quick-fix` | Run a bounded correction path |
   126	| `epic-cleanup` | Execute cleanup-only corrections before epic verification |
   127	| `epic-verify` | Launch epic-level verification |
   128	| `epic-synthesize` | Consolidate epic verifier findings |
   129	
   130	#### Shared CLI Result Envelope
   131	
   132	| Field | Description |
   133	|-------|-------------|
   134	| command | The public CLI command that produced the result |
   135	| version | Envelope version |
   136	| status | `ok`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked`, or `error` |
   137	| outcome | Command-specific routing outcome |
   138	| result | Command payload |
   139	| errors | Machine-readable errors |
   140	| warnings | Non-blocking warnings |
   141	| artifacts | Persisted artifact paths associated with the command |
   142	
   143	#### Implementation Log Requirements
   144	
   145	The log must record:
   146	
   147	- current run state and overall run status
   148	- active provider/model choices and verification-gate configuration
   149	- story sequence, dependencies, and current progress across stories
   150	- artifact paths used by the run
   151	- story-level checkpoints with evidence references, gate results, commit outcomes, and open items
   152	- cumulative test progression and regression-relevant baseline information
   153	- pre-epic-cleanup status
   154	- epic-verification and synthesis status
   155	- enough state for the orchestrator to resume after interruption without reconstructing the run from conversation context
   156	
   157	**Relevant Tech Design Sections**
   158	
   159	- `tech-design.md` -> System View, External Contracts, Module Architecture, Manifest and Build Metadata
   160	- `tech-design-skill-process.md` -> Orchestrator-Owned Artifacts, `team-impl-log.md`, Flow 1
   161	- `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` -> Flow 1: Inspect and Preflight, Tech Design Companion Discovery Rules
   162	
   163	*See the tech design document for full architecture, implementation targets, and test mapping.*
   164	
   165	### Definition of Done
   166	<!-- Jira: Definition of Done or Acceptance Criteria footer -->
   167	
   168	- `inspect` can distinguish valid two-file and four-file spec-pack layouts
   169	- Invalid layouts and missing artifacts return explicit blocking feedback
   170	- `team-impl-log.md` can be initialized for new runs and reused for existing runs
   171	- Public prompt-insert file detection is explicit and non-blocking when files are absent
   172	- Story 0 test fixtures cover valid, invalid, and resume-path spec-pack setups

27:The orchestrator starts with `SKILL.md`, resolves the spec-pack location, performs the minimum initial checks, and only then reads the reading-journey file. That file tells the orchestrator what to read from the spec pack and what to extract into durable state before deeper runtime work begins.

136:### `team-impl-log.md`
138:`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.

266:## Flow 1: Skill Load, Startup Orientation, and Basic Setup
270:This flow starts when the user explicitly asks Claude Code to load `ls-claude-impl`. The skill’s job at this stage is to orient the orchestrator quickly, resolve the spec-pack root, confirm the minimum required files exist, initialize the orchestrator-owned artifacts, and tell the orchestrator what to read next. The skill should not dump the full methodology immediately. It should get the orchestrator onto the rails with minimal context cost.

298:| `team-impl-log.md` | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration state and receipts |
304:| TC-1.3a | initializes new run log when absent | `inspect` + skill build tests | fixture spec pack without log | skill/setup path creates `team-impl-log.md` guidance and CLI reports ready for new run |
305:| TC-1.3b | resumes existing run log | `inspect` + fixture tests | fixture spec pack with existing log | setup path reports resume behavior and does not restart from scratch |
306:| TC-1.5a | full story inventory recorded before start | `inspect` contract tests | multiple stories in fixture | story order is returned and intended for log recording |
310:| TC-6.3a | resume after interruption | process-playbook tests | pre-existing log and config files | skill surface resumes from durable artifacts |
311:| TC-6.3b | resume after context stripping | process-playbook tests | no prior conversation context | guidance relies only on files on disk |
317:Once the spec-pack root is known and the basic files are present, the orchestrator needs a deliberate reading path. The reading journey exists to prevent both under-reading and over-reading. If the orchestrator reads everything up front, it burns context and weakens later attention. If it reads too little, it makes routing decisions without understanding the feature.
329:    participant Log as team-impl-log.md
348:- the presence of prompt insert files
367:The orchestrator is responsible for durable state, not just command dispatch. That means the skill surface must explicitly teach when to write receipts, when to update baselines, when to materialize cleanup items, and how to resume after interruption. If that behavior is not written into the skill clearly, the orchestrator will improvise, and the run will drift.
369:This feature’s recovery model is intentionally boring. The orchestrator resumes from disk, not from memory. It reads `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any command result artifacts that the CLI previously returned. That is the full contract. No hidden runtime state in the CLI should be required to understand what story is active, what verification round is current, or what cleanup remains.

14:The index stays small enough to be navigable while still recording the cross-cutting decisions that both companion docs inherit. The companion split follows the real boundary in this feature: one side teaches the orchestrator how the process works, the other side executes the process through a clean agent-first CLI.
18:The epic is designable. Its major flows, ACs, and TCs describe a coherent system with clear public seams: spec-pack discovery, run configuration, prompt assembly, retained implementation sessions, fresh verification, cleanup, and epic closeout. The epic is unusually detailed for a skill feature, but the added specificity is appropriate because the feature is not just a prose skill; it is a skill plus a runtime helper plus a prompt system plus a result-contract surface.

The CLI may emit continuation/session handles for provider sessions, but the orchestrator owns persistence, routing, and recovery through `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any future orchestrator-managed support files. | Resolved — clarified |

55:The problem being solved is reliability of implementation orchestration, not lack of methodology. Existing implementation skill surfaces carry useful process ideas, but they rely on too much live runtime improvisation and too many layers between the orchestrator and the actual work. The new design narrows the system to three stable public surfaces. First, the skill teaches the orchestrator the process and the reading journey. Second, the orchestrator owns state and decision-making. Third, the CLI executes bounded operations and returns disciplined machine-readable results. The center of gravity shifts away from “agent teams all the way down” and toward “one orchestrator with explicit process docs and a legible execution surface.”

    61	Finally, the repo’s existing build pipeline constrains how the feature lands. The current build emits markdown skill directories plus standalone markdown packs. It does not yet package executable runtime files into skill outputs. The design therefore includes build-system changes as part of the feature instead of leaving them to Story 0 discovery. The packaged skill must contain everything the orchestrator needs at runtime: markdown guidance plus a single bundled Node-compatible CLI file.

   105	### External Contracts
   106	
   107	The external contracts are narrower than the whole feature. They are the fixed points the orchestrator and CLI must agree on before implementation begins.
   108	
   109	#### Incoming Contracts
   110	
   111	| Input | Required | Source | Purpose |
   112	|---|---|---|---|
   113	| `epic.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Functional source of truth |
   114	| `tech-design.md` | yes | spec-pack root | Design index consumed by the orchestrator and some CLI operations |
   115	| `test-plan.md` | yes | spec-pack root | TC→test mapping and verification strategy |
   116	| `tech-design-*.md` companions | conditional | spec-pack root | Required only when the tech-design config is 4-doc |
   117	| `stories/` | yes | spec-pack root | Ordered story slices for implementation |
   118	| `impl-run.config.json` | yes | orchestrator-authored | Role/harness/model configuration for runtime operations |
   119	| `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for implementor prompt assembly |
   120	| `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` | no | spec-pack root | Optional public insert for verifier prompt assembly |
   121	
   122	#### Outgoing Contracts
   123	
   124	| Output | Producer | Destination | Purpose |
   125	|---|---|---|---|
   126	| `team-impl-log.md` updates | orchestrator | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration record and recovery state |
   127	| Structured command result JSON | CLI | stdout / orchestrator ingest | Machine-readable completion surface for each operation |
   128	| Prompt-ready execution payloads | CLI | secondary harnesses | Stable provider-facing prompt contract |
   129	| Bundled CLI artifact | build surface | packaged skill output | Runtime executable shipped with the skill |
   130	
   131	#### Outcome States
   132	
   133	The CLI should use explicit outcome states instead of ambiguous free-text status messages.
   134	
   135	| Operation Class | Allowed Outcome States |
   136	|---|---|
   137	| `inspect`, `preflight` | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` |
   138	| implementation / quick-fix operations | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` |
   139	| verifier batches | `pass`, `revise`, `block` |
   140	| cleanup operations | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |
   141	| synthesis operations | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |
   142	
   143	#### Runtime Prerequisites
   144	
   145	| Prerequisite | Where Needed | How to Verify |
   146	|---|---|---|
   147	| Bun 1.3.x+ | local development, build, tests | `bun --version` |
   148	| Node-compatible runtime | packaged CLI execution | `node -v` |
   149	| Git repository root | CLI commands that inspect repo/worktree context | `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` |
   150	| `codex` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: "codex"` | `codex --version` plus auth/smoke command |
   151	| `copilot` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: "copilot"` | `copilot --version` plus auth/smoke command |
   152	| `claude` CLI | always, because primary harness is Claude Code | `claude --version` plus auth/smoke command |

   252	The key boundary is that the orchestrator uses the skill to understand what should happen, then uses the CLI to make one bounded thing happen, then writes the result into its own durable state. The CLI does not own orchestration lifecycle between calls.

   255	
   256	The build surface needs one new manifest-level concept: a bundled runtime artifact that belongs inside the packaged skill even though it does not belong inside the composed markdown body.

   258	Proposed shape:
   259	
   260	```json

130	   - epic synthesizer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`
   131	   Story implementor still falls back to `secondary_harness: "none"` because v1 retained implementor continuation is not defined for Copilot.

   134	This keeps config authorship with the orchestrator while still satisfying the epic’s requirement that defaults and fallback behavior be resolved deterministically before implementation begins.
   135	
   136	### `team-impl-log.md`
   137	
   138	`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.
   139	
   140	Required sections:
   141	
   142	| Section | Purpose |
   143	|---|---|
   144	| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |
   145	| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |
   146	| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |
   147	| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |
   148	| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |
   149	| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |
   150	| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |
   151	| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |
   152	
   153	The log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. The orchestrator needs to read and update it directly. A second support file can be added later if highly structured state becomes necessary, but the v1 feature should keep the required surface small.
   154	
   155	### State Enum
   156	
   157	The log should carry one explicit current-state field using this enum:
   158	
   159	| State | Meaning |
   160	|---|---|
   161	| `SETUP` | skill loaded, spec-pack root being resolved, config/log initialization not complete |
   162	| `BETWEEN_STORIES` | ready to start the next story |
   163	| `STORY_ACTIVE` | implementation / verification / fix routing in progress for one story |
   164	| `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY` | all stories accepted, cleanup and epic closeout not yet complete |
   165	| `EPIC_VERIFY_ACTIVE` | cleanup, epic verifier batch, or synthesis is in progress |
   166	| `COMPLETE` | run complete |
   167	| `FAILED` | blocked or failed terminally pending human decision |
   168

   235	
   236	### Story Receipt Contract
   237	
   238	Each accepted story should record:
   239	
   240	- story id and title
   241	- implementor result path
   242	- verifier result paths
   243	- final story gate command + pass/fail outcome
   244	- dispositions for each unresolved finding (`fixed`, `accepted-risk`, `defer`)
   245	- cumulative test baseline before and after the story
   246	- any active continuation handles that remain relevant
   247	
   248	## Verification Gate Discovery
   249	
   250	Gate discovery must follow a fixed precedence order so the orchestrator does not improvise.
   251	
   252	1. Explicit CLI flags supplied for the current run
   253	2. Explicit entries in `impl-run.config.json` if later added
   254	3. package scripts in the repo root
   255	4. repository policy docs (`AGENTS.md`, `README`, related process docs)
   256	5. CI configuration as supporting evidence
   257	6. if still ambiguous: pause with `needs-user-decision`
   258	
   259	The playbook should teach the orchestrator to record:
   260	
   261	- the chosen story gate command
   262	- the chosen epic gate command
   263	- the source from which each was derived
   264	- any ambiguity note that required human confirmation
   265	
   266	## Flow 1: Skill Load, Startup Orientation, and Basic Setup
   267	
   268	**Covers:** AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6, AC-2.4, AC-6.3
   269	
   270	This flow starts when the user explicitly asks Claude Code to load `ls-claude-impl`. The skill’s job at this stage is to orient the orchestrator quickly, resolve the spec-pack root, confirm the minimum required files exist, initialize the orchestrator-owned artifacts, and tell the orchestrator what to read next. The skill should not dump the full methodology immediately. It should get the orchestrator onto the rails with minimal context cost.
   271	
   272	The first pass is intentionally shallow. It asks: “Do we have the right folder? Can we initialize durable state? What should the orchestrator read next?” The second pass, driven by the reading journey and CLI preflight, asks the deeper question: “Is the run actually executable?”
   273	
   274	```mermaid
   275	sequenceDiagram
   276	    participant User
   277	    participant Orchestrator as Claude Code Orchestrator
   278	    participant Skill as ls-claude-impl SKILL.md
   279	    participant SpecPack as Spec-Pack Root
   280	    participant Log as team-impl-log.md
   281	
   282	    Note over User,Orchestrator: AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6
   283	    User->>Orchestrator: "Load ls-claude-impl"
   284	    Orchestrator->>Skill: Load startup instructions
   285	    Skill-->>Orchestrator: Resolve spec-pack root + initial checks
   286	    Orchestrator->>SpecPack: Confirm epic / tech-design / test-plan / stories presence
   287	    Orchestrator->>Log: Create or resume durable run log
   288	    Skill-->>Orchestrator: Read claude-impl-reading-journey.md next
   289	```
   290	
   291	### Deliverables Created in This Flow
   292	
   293	| What | Where | Purpose |
   294	|---|---|---|
   295	| Skill entrypoint | `src/phases/claude-impl.md` | Startup orientation + next-step dispatch |
   296	| Reading-journey reference | `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md` | Controlled next read after setup |
   297	| `impl-run.config.json` | spec-pack root | Run configuration owned by orchestrator |
   298	| `team-impl-log.md` | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration state and receipts |
   299	
   300	### TC Mapping for This Flow
   301	
   302	| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |
   303	|---|---|---|---|---|
   304	| TC-1.3a | initializes new run log when absent | `inspect` + skill build tests | fixture spec pack without log | skill/setup path creates `team-impl-log.md` guidance and CLI reports ready for new run |
   305	| TC-1.3b | resumes existing run log | `inspect` + fixture tests | fixture spec pack with existing log | setup path reports resume behavior and does not restart from scratch |
   306	| TC-1.5a | full story inventory recorded before start | `inspect` contract tests | multiple stories in fixture | story order is returned and intended for log recording |
   307	| TC-1.6a | verification gates discovered and recorded | reading-journey/playbook tests | repo fixture with policy docs/scripts | skill guidance and initialization flow require gate capture before implementation |
   308	| TC-1.6b | ambiguous gate policy pauses setup | playbook tests | fixture with missing/ambiguous gate commands | skill surface directs user-resolution pause before execution |
   309	| TC-2.4a | run configuration recorded | skill + CLI integration tests | valid config + preflight result | orchestrator guidance records validated configuration in log |
   310	| TC-6.3a | resume after interruption | process-playbook tests | pre-existing log and config files | skill surface resumes from durable artifacts |
   311	| TC-6.3b | resume after context stripping | process-playbook tests | no prior conversation context | guidance relies only on files on disk |
   312	
   313	## Flow 2: Progressive Reading Journey
   314	
   315	**Covers:** AC-1.5, AC-3.4
   316	
   317	Once the spec-pack root is known and the basic files are present, the orchestrator needs a deliberate reading path. The reading journey exists to prevent both under-reading and over-reading. If the orchestrator reads everything up front, it burns context and weakens later attention. If it reads too little, it makes routing decisions without understanding the feature.
   318	
   319	The reading-journey reference should therefore do three things. It should define the read order. It should say what to extract into durable state after each read. And it should explicitly delay lower-value reads until they are needed. The orchestrator should come out of the journey with a stable mental model of the story queue, the tech-design shape, the verification gates, and the current run configuration.
   320

422	## Flow 1: Inspect and Preflight
   423	
   424	**Covers:** AC-1.1, AC-1.2, AC-1.4, AC-1.5, AC-1.6, AC-2.1 to AC-2.4
   425	
   426	This is the CLI’s setup boundary. `inspect` answers the structural question: is the spec pack arranged in a way the runtime understands? `preflight` answers the executable question: can this specific machine run the requested harness/model configuration with the required prompt assets and files in place?
   427	
   428	This split matters. The orchestrator should be able to inspect a spec pack before it has chosen or authored a run config, but it should not launch any provider-backed work until preflight has passed.
   429	
   430	```mermaid
   431	sequenceDiagram
   432	    participant Orchestrator
   433	    participant CLI
   434	    participant SpecPack
   435	    participant Config as impl-run.config.json
   436	    participant Providers as Provider CLIs
   437	
   438	    Note over Orchestrator,Providers: AC-1.1 to AC-2.4
   439	    Orchestrator->>CLI: inspect --spec-pack-root <path>
   440	    CLI->>SpecPack: Resolve required files, story order, insert presence
   441	    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Spec-pack summary
   442	    Orchestrator->>CLI: preflight --spec-pack-root <path>
   443	    CLI->>Config: Parse and validate run config
   444	    CLI->>Providers: Validate requested harness availability
   445	    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Readiness report + validated provider matrix
   446	```
   447	
   448	### Inspect Result Contract
   449	
   450	```typescript
   451	export interface InspectResult {
   452	  status: "ready" | "needs-user-decision" | "blocked";
   453	  specPackRoot: string;
   454	  techDesignShape: "two-file" | "four-file";
   455	  artifacts: {
   456	    epicPath: string;
   457	    techDesignPath: string;
   458	    techDesignCompanionPaths: string[];
   459	    testPlanPath: string;
   460	    storiesDir: string;
   461	  };
   462	  stories: Array<{ id: string; title: string; path: string; order: number }>;
   463	  inserts: {
   464	    customStoryImplPromptInsert: "present" | "absent";
   465	    customStoryVerifierPromptInsert: "present" | "absent";
   466	  };
   467	  blockers: string[];
   468	  notes: string[];
   469	}
   470	```
   471	
   472	### Preflight Result Contract
   473	
   474	```typescript
   475	export interface PreflightResult {
   476	  status: "ready" | "needs-user-decision" | "blocked";
   477	  validatedConfig: ImplRunConfig;
   478	  providerMatrix: ProviderMatrix;
   479	  verificationGates?: {
   480	    storyGate: string;
   481	    epicGate: string;
   482	    source: string;
   483	  };
   484	  configValidationNotes: string[];
   485	  promptAssets: {
   486	    basePromptsReady: boolean;
   487	    snippetsReady: boolean;
   488	    notes: string[];
   489	  };
   490	  blockers: string[];
   491	  notes: string[];
   492	}
   493	```
   494	
   495	`preflight` validates the authored config and reports the consequences of that config. It does **not** generate or rewrite the default matrix in v1. Default selection is part of the skill/process contract and is owned by the orchestrator before preflight runs.
   496	
   497	### Story Ordering and Story ID Rules
   498	
   499	Spec-pack resolution must not invent story order heuristically.
   500	
   501	1. Read markdown files from `stories/`, excluding `coverage.md`.
   502	2. Derive `storyId` from the filename without extension.
   503	3. Order stories by the leading numeric prefix in the filename when present.
   504	4. If two files have the same numeric prefix, fall back to lexical filename order.
   505	5. If no numeric prefixes exist anywhere, use lexical filename order and emit a warning in `inspect`.
   506	6. If the naming pattern is mixed and produces ambiguous ordering, return `needs-user-decision`.
   507	
   508	This keeps story order reproducible and makes the ordering rules explicit for both runtime code and tests.
   509	
   510	Examples:
   511	
   512	- `00-foundation.md`, `01-run-setup.md`, `02-prompts.md` -> valid, ordered numerically
   513	- `01-a.md`, `01-b.md` -> valid, ordered lexically within the same prefix
   514	- `01-a.md`, `b.md`, `02-c.md` -> ambiguous mixed pattern, return `needs-user-decision`
   515	
   516	### Tech Design Companion Discovery Rules
   517	
   518	The four-file tech-design shape is valid only when:
   519	
   520	- `tech-design.md` exists
   521	- `test-plan.md` exists
   522	- exactly two additional files matching `tech-design-*.md` exist
   523	- `tech-design.md` itself is excluded from the companion count
   524	- companion paths are sorted lexically before being returned
   525	
   526	Invalid examples:
   527	
   528	- one companion file only
   529	- three or more companion files
   530	- unrelated files such as `tech-design-notes.md` or `tech-design-archive.md` inside the spec-pack root
   531	
   532	These should return `blocked` with an explicit shape error rather than being ignored silently.
   533

   555	
   556	## Flow 2: Story Implementation and Continuation
   557	
   558	**Covers:** AC-3.1, AC-3.2, AC-3.4a, AC-4.1 to AC-4.5
   559	
   560	`story-implement` launches the implementor for one story using the assembled prompt and the configured role assignment. The CLI owns prompt assembly, provider invocation, self-review prompt evolution, and the returned structured result. It does not own story acceptance. `story-continue` exists because the story implementor may keep working in the same provider session across follow-up fixes or post-review continuation.

item.text: I’ve got the contract in hand now. Next I’m checking whether the runtime and tests on disk really implement just the Story-0 foundation surface, and whether the claimed coverage is exercising real code paths rather than documentation-only or mock-shaped success.

1	import { join, resolve } from "node:path";
     2	
     3	import { pathExists, readTextFile, writeTextFile } from "./fs-utils";
     4	
     5	export const TEAM_IMPL_LOG_FILE_NAME = "team-impl-log.md";
     6	
     7	export interface EnsureTeamImplLogInput {
     8	  specPackRoot: string;
     9	  storyIds: string[];
    10	}
    11	
    12	export interface EnsureTeamImplLogResult {
    13	  created: boolean;
    14	  path: string;
    15	  content: string;
    16	}
    17	
    18	export function renderTeamImplLogTemplate(input: EnsureTeamImplLogInput): string {
    19	  const specPackRoot = resolve(input.specPackRoot);
    20	  const storyLines =
    21	    input.storyIds.length === 0
    22	      ? "- none yet"
    23	      : input.storyIds.map((storyId) => `- ${storyId}`).join("\n");
    24	
    25	  return [
    26	    "# Team Implementation Log",
    27	    "",
    28	    "## Run Overview",
    29	    "- State: SETUP",
    30	    `- Spec Pack Root: ${specPackRoot}`,
    31	    "- Current Story: not-started",
    32	    "- Current Phase: inspect",
    33	    "",
    34	    "## Run Configuration",
    35	    "- Primary Harness: claude-code",
    36	    "- Story Implementor: pending",
    37	    "- Quick Fixer: pending",
    38	    "- Story Verifier 1: pending",
    39	    "- Story Verifier 2: pending",
    40	    "- Self Review Passes: pending",
    41	    "- Degraded Diversity: pending",
    42	    "",
    43	    "## Verification Gates",
    44	    "- Story Gate: pending",
    45	    "- Epic Gate: pending",
    46	    "- Gate Discovery Source: pending",
    47	    "",
    48	    "## Story Sequence",
    49	    storyLines,
    50	    "",
    51	    "## Current Continuation Handles",
    52	    "- none",
    53	    "",
    54	    "## Story Receipts",
    55	    "- none yet",
    56	    "",
    57	    "## Cumulative Baselines",
    58	    "- Baseline Before Current Story: pending",
    59	    "- Expected After Current Story: pending",
    60	    "- Latest Actual Total: pending",
    61	    "",
    62	    "## Cleanup / Epic Verification",
    63	    "- Cleanup Artifact: pending",
    64	    "- Cleanup Status: not-started",
    65	    "- Epic Verification Status: not-started",
    66	    "",
    67	    "## Open Risks / Accepted Risks",
    68	    "- none",
    69	    "",
    70	  ].join("\n");
    71	}
    72	
    73	export async function ensureTeamImplLog(
    74	  input: EnsureTeamImplLogInput
    75	): Promise<EnsureTeamImplLogResult> {
    76	  const path = join(resolve(input.specPackRoot), TEAM_IMPL_LOG_FILE_NAME);
    77	
    78	  if (await pathExists(path)) {
    79	    return {
    80	      created: false,
    81	      path,
    82	      content: await readTextFile(path),
    83	    };
    84	  }
    85	
    86	  const content = renderTeamImplLogTemplate(input);
    87	  await writeTextFile(path, content);
    88	
    89	  return {
    90	    created: true,
    91	    path,
    92	    content,
    93	  };
    94	}

1	# Claude Implementation
     2	
     3	Use `ls-claude-impl` when a completed spec pack already exists and Claude Code needs a disciplined implementation workflow with a bundled bounded-operation CLI.
     4	
     5	## Startup Orientation
     6	
     7	Resolve the spec-pack root before reading deeply. The minimum v1 layout is:
     8	
     9	- `epic.md`
    10	- `tech-design.md`
    11	- `test-plan.md`
    12	- `stories/`
    13	
    14	Run `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root <path> --json` as the first runtime check. Capture the returned tech-design shape, required artifacts, story inventory, and insert status before you move on to run configuration or provider-backed work.
    15	
    16	## Initialization Steps
    17	
    18	1. Confirm the spec-pack root and run `inspect`.
    19	2. If `team-impl-log.md` is absent, create `team-impl-log.md` and initialize the run in `SETUP`.
    20	3. If `team-impl-log.md` already exists, read it first and resume from the recorded state.
    21	4. Record whether the spec pack is a two-file or four-file tech-design layout.
    22	5. Record whether `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md` and `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md` are present. Their absence is non-blocking.
    23	6. Discover the story gate and epic gate before implementation starts. If the policy stays ambiguous, pause for a user decision instead of improvising.
    24	
    25	## State Ownership
    26	
    27	- `impl-run.config.json` is orchestrator-owned configuration.
    28	- `team-impl-log.md` is the durable run record.
    29	- The CLI performs one bounded operation per call and returns structured results.
    30	- The CLI does not own story progression, acceptance, or recovery strategy between calls.
    31	
    32	## Read Next
    33	
    34	After the initial `inspect` pass and log setup, read:
    35	
    36	1. `references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md`
    37	2. `references/claude-impl-process-playbook.md`
    38	3. `references/claude-impl-cli-operations.md`
    39	4. `references/claude-impl-prompt-system.md`

1	# Claude Impl Reading Journey
     2	
     3	Use this file after the initial `inspect` pass. The goal is to read just enough, in the right order, to establish durable state before any implementation work begins.
     4	
     5	## Read Order
     6	
     7	1. Read `tech-design.md`.
     8	2. If `inspect` reported a four-file layout, read `tech-design-skill-process.md` and `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` next.
     9	3. Read `test-plan.md`.
    10	4. Read `epic.md`.
    11	5. Read every story file in `stories/` in order before starting Story 0 or Story 1.
    12	
    13	## What To Record In `team-impl-log.md`
    14	
    15	- the resolved spec-pack root
    16	- whether the tech design is a two-file or four-file pack
    17	- the ordered story inventory
    18	- the story and epic verification gates
    19	- any blockers that require a user decision
    20	- whether public prompt inserts are active
    21	
    22	## Prompt Insert Detection
    23	
    24	Check for these public insert files in the spec-pack root:
    25	
    26	- `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md`
    27	- `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md`
    28	
    29	If either file is present, record that it is available for later prompt assembly. If neither insert file is present, continue normally and record that no public prompt inserts are active.

1	# Claude Impl Process Playbook
     2	
     3	This is the main process reference after setup. Use it to decide which bounded CLI operation comes next and what evidence must be written back into `team-impl-log.md`.
     4	
     5	## Stage Map
     6	
     7	1. Setup: resolve the spec pack, initialize or resume `team-impl-log.md`, and discover verification gates.
     8	2. Preflight: validate the authored run configuration before any provider-backed work starts.
     9	3. Story cycle: implement, verify, route fixes, then record the story receipt.
    10	4. Closeout: complete cleanup, epic verification, synthesis, and the final orchestrator-owned gate.
    11	
    12	## Setup Rules
    13	
    14	- Use `inspect` for structural discovery.
    15	- Do not start implementation until the story gate and epic gate are recorded.
    16	- If gate policy is ambiguous after checking project policy docs and package scripts, pause with a user decision instead of guessing.
    17	- Resume from disk artifacts, not conversation memory.
    18	
    19	## Durable Artifacts
    20	
    21	- `impl-run.config.json`: the run shape chosen by the orchestrator
    22	- `team-impl-log.md`: the durable record of state, gates, receipts, and risks
    23	- CLI result artifacts under `artifacts/`: machine-readable evidence returned by each bounded operation
    24	
    25	## Story Receipt Minimum
    26	
    27	Before accepting a story, record:
    28	
    29	- the story id and title
    30	- the command result artifacts that justify progression
    31	- the exact story gate command and result
    32	- the disposition of every unresolved finding
    33	- the cumulative baseline before and after the story
    34	
    35	## Recovery Rule
    36	
    37	If execution is interrupted, restart from the files on disk: `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and the command result artifacts already written under `artifacts/`.

1	# Claude Impl CLI Operations
     2	
     3	This guide explains the public CLI from the orchestrator's perspective. The CLI handles one bounded operation at a time and returns a structured envelope that the orchestrator records in `team-impl-log.md`.
     4	
     5	## Command Map
     6	
     7	| Command | Use It When | Outcome States | What To Do Next |
     8	|---|---|---|---|
     9	| `inspect` | You need to confirm the spec-pack layout, tech-design shape, story inventory, and insert presence. | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` | Record the layout and continue to the reading journey or pause for a user decision. |
    10	| `preflight` | The reading journey is complete and `impl-run.config.json` exists. | `ready`, `needs-user-decision`, `blocked` | Record the validated configuration and gate choices before story work. |
    11	| `story-implement` | A story is ready to start. | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` | Route verification or follow-up work based on the returned outcome. |
    12	| `story-continue` | The active retained implementor session should continue. | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` | Continue the same story session only when the continuation handle matches. |
    13	| `story-verify` | Fresh verifiers should review one story. | `pass`, `revise`, `block` | Route fixes or move toward acceptance. |
    14	| `quick-fix` | A small, bounded fix should run without restarting the full implementor workflow. | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-more-routing`, `blocked` | Re-verify or route a larger follow-up path. |
    15	| `epic-cleanup` | Approved cleanup-only fixes should run before epic verification. | `cleaned`, `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` | Review the cleanup result before epic verification continues. |
    16	| `epic-verify` | All stories are accepted and cleanup is complete. | `pass`, `revise`, `block` | Gather the verifier batch for synthesis. |
    17	| `epic-synthesize` | Epic verifier results are available and need consolidation. | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` | Run the final orchestrator-owned gate or route more work. |
    18	
    19	## IO Contract
    20	
    21	- With `--json`, stdout must contain exactly one JSON envelope.
    22	- The same envelope is persisted under `<spec-pack-root>/artifacts/`.
    23	- `stderr` is for progress or debugging only; it is not the routing source of truth.
    24	
    25	## Ownership Boundary
    26	
    27	The final story gate and final epic gate remain orchestrator-owned. The CLI can report readiness, verification findings, and cleanup outcomes, but it does not accept stories or close the epic by itself.

1	# Claude Impl Prompt System
     2	
     3	The runtime prompt model is intentionally simple from the orchestrator's perspective:
     4	
     5	1. choose the role
     6	2. load the role's base prompt
     7	3. append the required shared snippets
     8	4. append any optional public inserts
     9	5. interpolate runtime values such as story id, tech-design shape, and gate commands
    10	
    11	## Public Insert Files
    12	
    13	The spec-pack root may contain these optional public inserts:
    14	
    15	- `custom-story-impl-prompt-insert.md`
    16	- `custom-story-verifier-prompt-insert.md`
    17	
    18	These files are optional public inserts. `inspect` records whether they are present, and later prompt assembly uses them when the matching role runs. Their absence is non-blocking and should be recorded as inactive rather than treated as an error.
    19	
    20	## Mental Model
    21	
    22	- implementor prompts use the implementation base prompt plus the required snippets and the optional implementor insert
    23	- verifier prompts use the verifier base prompt plus the required snippets and the optional verifier insert
    24	- prompt assembly stays deterministic so the orchestrator can reason about what the runtime will send

src/phases/story.md:58:The default for Story 0 is foundation-only with no TDD cycle -- types and fixtures don't need test-driven development. However, projects may pragmatically include foundational endpoints or smoke tests that verify the infrastructure actually works. A health check endpoint that proves the server starts and dependencies connect is a reasonable Story 0 addition. The key constraint is that Story 0 establishes what all subsequent stories depend on -- it's the shared foundation, not a feature delivery.

src/phases/story.md:64:- Skip smoke tests in Story 0 when they would validate feature behavior better covered by Story 1+ TDD.
src/phases/story.md:65:- Keep Story 0 smoke coverage minimal; this is a foundation check, not feature verification.

src/templates/tech-design.template.md:635:`green-verify` exists because Red tests are the behavioral contract for Green. Test files should not be modified during Green implementation. `green-verify` runs the standard verification pipeline then checks that no test files were changed.
src/templates/tech-design.template.md:637:If integration or e2e suites don't exist yet, `verify-all` should still exist as a wirable command from day one. The placeholder should output a clear notice that the suite isn't implemented yet (e.g., "SKIP: integration suite not yet implemented") and exit successfully so CI doesn't break — but never silently pass as if verification occurred.

The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.\n   139\t\n   140\tRequired sections:\n   141\t\n   142\t| Section | Purpose |\n   143\t|---|---|\n   144\t| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |\n   145\t| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |\n   146\t| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |\n   147\t| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |\n   148\t| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |\n   149\t| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |\n   150\t| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |\n   151\t| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |\n   152\t\n   153\tThe log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. 157\tThe log should carry one explicit current-state field using this enum:\n   158\t\n   159\t| State | Meaning |\n   160\t|---|---|\n   161\t| `SETUP` | skill loaded, spec-pack root being resolved, verify-all\n   194\t- Gate Discovery Source: package.json scripts\n   195\t\n   196\t## Story Sequence\n   197\t- story-00-foundation\n   198\t- story-01-run-setup\n   199\t- story-02-prompt-composition\n   200\t\n   201\t## Current Continuation Handles\n   202\t- Story Implementor:\n   203\t  - Story: story-03\n   204\t  - Provider: codex\n   205\t  - Session ID: abc123\n   206\t\n   207\t## Story Receipts\n   208\t### story-02\n   209\t- Implementor Evidence Ref: artifacts/story-02/implement-result.json\n   210\t- Verifier Evidence Refs:\n   211\t  - artifacts/story-02/verify-primary.json\n   212\t  - artifacts/story-02/verify-secondary.json\n   213\t- Gate Result: pass\n   214\t- Dispositions:\n   215\t  - FIX-1: fixed\n   216\t  - RISK-2: accepted-risk\n   217\t- Open Risks:\n   218\t  - none\n   219\t\n   220\t## Cumulative Baselines\n   221\t- Baseline Before Current Story: 48\n   222\t- Expected After Current Story: 56\n   223\t- Latest Actual Total: 56\n   224\t\n   225\t## Cleanup / Epic Verification\n   226\t- Cleanup Artifact: artifacts/cleanup/cleanup-batch.md\n   227\t- Cleanup Status: not-started\n   228\t- Epic Verification Status: not-started\n   229\t\n   230\t## Open Risks / Accepted Risks\n   231\t- none\n   232\t```\n   233\t\n   234\tThe orchestrator can add prose beneath these headings, but these sections and labels should be present so recovery is deterministic.\n   235\t\n   236\t### Story Receipt Contract\n   237\t\n   238\tEach accepted story should record:\n   239\t\n   240\t- story id and title\n   241\t- implementor result path\n   242\t- verifier result paths\n   243\t- final story gate command + pass/fail outcome\n   244\t- dispositions for each unresolved finding (`fixed`, `accepted-risk`, `defer`)\n   245\t- cumulative test baseline before and after the story\n   246\t- any active continuation handles that remain relevant\n   247\t\n   248\t## Verification Gate Discovery\n   249\t\n   250\tGate discovery must follow a fixed precedence order so the orchestrator does not improvise.\n   251\t\n   252\t1. Explicit CLI flags supplied for the current run\n   253\t2. Explicit entries in `impl-run.config.json` if later added\n   254\t3. package scripts in the repo root\n   255\t4. repository policy docs (`AGENTS.md`, `README`, related process docs)\n   256\t5. CI configuration as supporting evidence\n   257\t6. if still ambiguous: pause with `needs-user-decision`\n   258\t\n   259\tThe playbook should teach the orchestrator to record:\n   260\t\n   261\t- the chosen story gate command\n   262\t- the chosen epic gate command\n   263\t- the source from which each was derived\n   264\t- any ambiguity note that required human confirmation\n   265\t\n   266\t## Flow 1: Skill Load, Startup Orientation, and Basic Setup\n   267\t\n   268\t**Covers:** AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6, AC-2.4, AC-6.3\n   269\t\n   270\tThis flow starts when the user explicitly asks Claude Code to load `ls-claude-impl`. The skill’s job at this stage is to orient the orchestrator quickly, resolve the spec-pack root, confirm the minimum required files exist, initialize the orchestrator-owned artifacts, and tell the orchestrator what to read next. The skill should not dump the full methodology immediately. It should get the orchestrator onto the rails with minimal context cost.\n   271\t\n   272\tThe first pass is intentionally shallow. It asks: “Do we have the right folder? Can we initialize durable state? What should the orchestrator read next?” The second pass, driven by the reading journey and CLI preflight, asks the deeper question: “Is the run actually executable?”\n   273\t\n   274\t```mermaid\n   275\tsequenceDiagram\n   276\t    participant User\n   277\t    participant Orchestrator as Claude Code Orchestrator\n   278\t    participant Skill as ls-claude-impl SKILL.md\n   279\t    participant SpecPack as Spec-Pack Root\n   280\t    participant Log as team-impl-log.md\n   281\t\n   282\t    Note over User,Orchestrator: AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6\n   283\t    User->>Orchestrator: \"Load ls-claude-impl\"\n   284\t    Orchestrator->>Skill: Load startup instructions\n   285\t    Skill-->>Orchestrator: Resolve spec-pack root + initial checks\n   286\t    Orchestrator->>SpecPack: Confirm epic / tech-design / test-plan / stories presence\n   287\t    Orchestrator->>Log: Create or resume durable run log\n   288\t    Skill-->>Orchestrator: Read claude-impl-reading-journey.md next\n   289\t```\n   290\t\n   291\t### Deliverables Created in This Flow\n   292\t\n   293\t| What | Where | Purpose |\n   294\t|---|---|---|\n   295\t| Skill entrypoint | `src/phases/claude-impl.md` | Startup orientation + next-step dispatch |\n   296\t| Reading-journey reference | `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md` | Controlled next read after setup |\n   297\t| `impl-run.config.json` | spec-pack root | Run configuration owned by orchestrator |\n   298\t| `team-impl-log.md` | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration state and receipts |\n   299\t\n   300\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\n   301\t\n   302\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\n   303\t|---|---|---|---|---|\n   304\t| TC-1.3a | initializes new run log when absent | `inspect` + skill build tests | fixture spec pack without log | skill/setup path creates `team-impl-log.md` guidance and CLI reports ready for new run |\n   305\t| TC-1.3b | resumes existing run log | `inspect` + fixture tests | fixture spec pack with existing log | setup path reports resume behavior and does not restart from scratch |\n   306\t| TC-1.5a | full story inventory recorded before start | `inspect` contract tests | multiple stories in fixture | story order is returned and intended for log recording |\n   307\t| TC-1.6a | verification gates discovered and recorded | reading-journey/playbook tests | repo fixture with policy docs/scripts | skill guidance and initialization flow require gate capture before implementation |\n   308\t| TC-1.6b | ambiguous gate policy pauses setup | playbook tests | fixture with missing/ambiguous gate commands | skill surface directs user-resolution pause before execution |\n   309\t| TC-2.4a | run configuration recorded | skill + CLI integration tests | valid config + preflight result | orchestrator guidance records validated configuration in log |\n   310\t| TC-6.3a | resume after interruption | process-playbook tests | pre-existing log and config files | skill surface resumes from durable artifacts |\n   311\t| TC-6.3b | resume after context stripping | process-playbook tests | no prior conversation context | guidance relies only on files on disk |\n   312\t\n   313\t## Flow 2: Progressive Reading Journey\n   314\t\n   315\t**Covers:** AC-1.5, AC-3.4\n   316\t\n   317\tOnce the spec-pack root is known and the basic files are present, the orchestrator needs a deliberate reading path. The reading journey exists to prevent both under-reading and over-reading. If the orchestrator reads everything up front, it burns context and weakens later attention. If it reads too little, it makes routing decisions without understanding the feature.\n   318\t\n   319\tThe reading-journey reference should therefore do three things. It should define the read order. It should say what to extract into durable state after each read. And it should explicitly delay lower-value reads until they are needed. The orchestrator should come out of the journey with a stable mental model of the story queue, the tech-design shape, the verification gates, and the current run configuration.\n   320\t\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

The orchestrator should be able to inspect a spec pack before it has chosen or authored a run config, but it should not launch any provider-backed work until preflight has passed.\n   429\t\n   430\t```mermaid\n   431\tsequenceDiagram\n   432\t    participant Orchestrator\n   433\t    participant CLI\n   434\t    participant SpecPack\n   435\t    participant Config as impl-run.config.json\n   436\t    participant Providers as Provider CLIs\n   437\t\n   438\t    Note over Orchestrator,Providers: AC-1.1 to AC-2.4\n   439\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: inspect --spec-pack-root <path>\n   440\t    CLI->>SpecPack: Resolve required files, story order, insert presence\n   441\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Spec-pack summary\n   442\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: preflight --spec-pack-root <path>\n   443\t    CLI->>Config: Parse and validate run config\n   444\t    CLI->>Providers: Validate requested harness availability\n   445\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Readiness report + validated provider matrix\n   446\t```\n   447\t\n   448\t### Inspect Result Contract\n   449\t\n   450\t```typescript\n   451\texport interface InspectResult {\n   452\t  status: \"ready\" | \"needs-user-decision\" | \"blocked\";\n   453\t  specPackRoot: string;\n   454\t  techDesignShape: \"two-file\" | \"four-file\";\n   455\t  artifacts: {\n   456\t    epicPath: string;\n   457\t    techDesignPath: string;\n   458\t    techDesignCompanionPaths: string[];\n   459\t    testPlanPath: string;\n   460\t    storiesDir: string;\n   461\t  };\n   462\t  stories: Array<{ id: string; title: string; path: string; order: number }>;\n   463\t  inserts: {\n   464\t    customStoryImplPromptInsert: \"present\" | \"absent\";\n   465\t    customStoryVerifierPromptInsert: \"present\" | \"absent\";\n   466\t  };\n   467\t  blockers: string[];\n   468\t  notes: string[];\n   469\t}\n   470\t```\n   471\t\n   472\t### Preflight Result Contract\n   473\t\n   474\t```typescript\n   475\texport interface PreflightResult {\n   476\t  status: \"ready\" | \"needs-user-decision\" | \"blocked\";\n   477\t  validatedConfig: ImplRunConfig;\n   478\t  providerMatrix: ProviderMatrix;\n   479\t  verificationGates?: {\n   480\t    storyGate: string;\n   481\t    epicGate: string;\n   482\t    source: string;\n   483\t  };\n   484\t  configValidationNotes: string[];\n   485\t  promptAssets: {\n   486\t    basePromptsReady: boolean;\n   487\t    snippetsReady: boolean;\n   488\t    notes: string[];\n   489\t  };\n   490\t  blockers: string[];\n   491\t  notes: string[];\n   492\t}\n   493\t```\n   494\t\n   495\t`preflight` validates the authored config and reports the consequences of that config. It does **not** generate or rewrite the default matrix in v1. Default selection is part of the skill/process contract and is owned by the orchestrator before preflight runs.\n   496\t\n   497\t### Story Ordering and Story ID Rules\n   498\t\n   499\tSpec-pack resolution must not invent story order heuristically.\n   500\t\n   501\t1. Read markdown files from `stories/`, excluding `coverage.md`.\n   502\t2. Derive `storyId` from the filename without extension.\n   503\t3. Order stories by the leading numeric prefix in the filename when present.\n   504\t4. If two files have the same numeric prefix, fall back to lexical filename order.\n   505\t5. If no numeric prefixes exist anywhere, use lexical filename order and emit a warning in `inspect`.\n   506\t6. If the naming pattern is mixed and produces ambiguous ordering, return `needs-user-decision`.\n   507\t\n   508\tThis keeps story order reproducible and makes the ordering rules explicit for both runtime code and tests.\n   509\t\n   510\tExamples:\n   511\t\n   512\t- `00-foundation.md`, `01-run-setup.md`, `02-prompts.md` -> valid, ordered numerically\n   513\t- `01-a.md`, `01-b.md` -> valid, ordered lexically within the same prefix\n   514\t- `01-a.md`, `b.md`, `02-c.md` -> ambiguous mixed pattern, return `needs-user-decision`\n   515\t\n   516\t### Tech Design Companion Discovery Rules\n   517\t\n   518\tThe four-file tech-design shape is valid only when:\n   519\t\n   520\t- `tech-design.md` exists\n   521\t- `test-plan.md` exists\n   522\t- exactly two additional files matching `tech-design-*.md` exist\n   523\t- `tech-design.md` itself is excluded from the companion count\n   524\t- companion paths are sorted lexically before being returned\n   525\t\n   526\tInvalid examples:\n   527\t\n   528\t- one companion file only\n   529\t- three or more companion files\n   530\t- unrelated files such as `tech-design-notes.md` or `tech-design-archive.md` inside the spec-pack root\n   531\t\n   532\tThese should return `blocked` with an explicit shape error rather than being ignored silently.\n   533\t\n   534\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\n   535\t\n   536\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\n   537\t|---|---|---|---|---|\n   538\t| TC-1.1a | accepts two-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture with `epic.md`, `tech-design.md`, `test-plan.md`, `stories/` | `inspect` returns `ready` and `two-file` |\n   539\t| TC-1.1b | accepts four-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | fixture with companions | `inspect` returns `ready` and `four-file` |\n   540\t| TC-1.1c | rejects `stories.md` layout | `inspect-command.test.ts` | invalid story layout fixture | `blocked` with explicit message |\n   541\t| TC-1.1d | rejects missing required artifact | `inspect-command.test.ts` | missing file fixture | `blocked` with named artifact |\n   542\t| TC-1.2a | records two-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | two-file fixture | tech-design shape recorded |\n   543\t| TC-1.2b | records four-file config | `inspect-command.test.ts` | four-file fixture | companions surfaced for later use |\n   544\t| TC-1.4a | detects both inserts | marked `absent`, no failure |\n   546\t| TC-1.5a | records full story order | `inspect-command.test.ts` | multi-story fixture | story inventory sorted and returned |\n   547\t| TC-1.6a | preflight carries discovered gates forward | `preflight-command.test.ts` | valid config + discovered gate inputs | readiness report includes gate-aware notes |\n   548\t| TC-1.6b | ambiguous gate policy returns `needs-user-decision` | `preflight-command.test.ts` | missing gate inputs | explicit pause state |\n   549\t| TC-2.1a | primary harness always available check runs | `preflight-command.test.ts` | Codex role |\n   551\t| TC-2.2b | Copilot accepted when Codex absent and Copilot chosen | `preflight-command.test.ts` | config using Copilot | provider matrix validates Copilot path |\n   552\t| TC-2.2c | roles using `none` fall back to primary harness only | `preflight-command.test.ts` | config with `none` | no missing-secondary-harness error |\n   553\t| TC-2.3a-e | role model matrix valid fixture | structured `validatedConfig` returned |\n   555\t\n   556\t## Flow 2: Story Implementation and Continuation\n   557\t\n   558\t**Covers:** AC-3.1, AC-3.2, AC-3.4a, AC-4.1 to AC-4.5\n   559\t\n   560\t`story-implement` launches the implementor for one story using the assembled prompt and the configured role assignment. The CLI owns prompt assembly, provider invocation, self-review prompt evolution, and the returned structured result. It does not own story acceptance. `story-continue` exists because the story implementor may keep working in the same provider session across follow-up fixes or post-review continuation.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-00/001-implementor.jsonl:18:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_9","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"nl -ba docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/tech-design.md | sed -n '1,450p'\"","aggregated_output":"     1\t# Technical Design: ls-claude-impl\n     2\t\n     3\t## Purpose\n     4\t\n     5\tThis design translates the `ls-claude-impl` epic into an implementable architecture for a new Claude Code orchestration skill plus a Node-compatible implementation CLI. The feature has two dense domains that need different levels of treatment: the orchestrator-facing skill surface and the CLI/runtime surface that executes bounded operations for multiple secondary harnesses. For that reason this design uses **Config B**:\n     6\t\n     7\t| Document | Purpose |\n     8\t|---|---|\n     9\t| `tech-design.md` | Decision record, context, system view, top-tier surfaces, module architecture overview, dependency and packaging decisions, work breakdown |\n    10\t| `tech-design-skill-process.md` | The orchestrator-facing skill surface: progressive disclosure docs, reading journey, state ownership, process playbook, stage transitions |\n    11\t| `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` | The CLI/runtime surface: command model, config schemas, prompt asset system, provider adapters, operation contracts, packaging |\n    12\t| `test-plan.md` | TC-to-test mapping, mock strategy, fixture strategy, test counts, chunk-level TDD plan |\n    13\t\n    14\tThe index stays small enough to be navigable while still recording the cross-cutting decisions that both companion docs inherit. The companion split follows the real boundary in this feature: one side teaches the orchestrator how the process works, the other side executes the process through a clean agent-first CLI.\n    15\t\n    16\t## Spec Validation\n    17\t\n    18\tThe epic is designable. Its major flows, ACs, and TCs describe a coherent system with clear public seams: spec-pack discovery, run configuration, prompt assembly, retained implementation sessions, fresh verification, cleanup, and epic closeout. The epic is unusually detailed for a skill feature, but the added specificity is appropriate because the feature is not just a prose skill; it is a skill plus a runtime helper plus a prompt system plus a result-contract surface.\n    19\t\n    20\tThe design does make three clarifications that should be recorded explicitly before implementation begins. None of them block design, but each one turns an implied behavior into a concrete contract the implementation can build around.\n    21\t\n    22\t| Issue | Spec Location | Resolution | Status |\n    23\t|---|---|---|---|\n    24\t| The epic requires the orchestrator to record role defaults and run choices, but it does not name a dedicated machine-readable run config file. | AC-2.4, Data Contracts, `team-impl-log.md` requirements | Introduce `impl-run.config.json` at the spec-pack root as the orchestrator-owned run configuration. `team-impl-log.md` remains the durable narrative/state record. | Resolved — clarified |\n    25\t| The epic distinguishes Codex/Copilot-backed GPT lanes from Claude-only fallback, but the harness terminology needs to preserve Claude Code as the always-present primary harness. | AC-2.2, AC-2.3, Role Model Matrix | Use `primary_harness: \"claude-code\"` plus per-role `secondary_harness: \"codex\" | \"copilot\" | \"none\"`. `none` means the role uses the primary harness only. | Resolved — clarified |\n    26\t| The epic says the CLI should be stateless across calls, but story implementation also requires retained implementor continuity. | Constraint C3, AC-4.2, AC-6.3 | Keep orchestration state outside the CLI. The CLI may emit continuation/session handles for provider sessions, but the orchestrator owns persistence, routing, and recovery through `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any future orchestrator-managed support files. | Resolved — clarified |\n    27\t| The epic allows Copilot as the generic GPT fallback, but v1 Copilot support is limited to fresh-session roles while story implementation requires retained continuation. | AC-2.2, AC-4.2, Role Model Matrix | In v1, disallow Copilot for `story_implementor`. Retained implementor fallback order is Codex -> Claude Code primary harness. Copilot remains valid for fresh verifier, quick-fix, and synthesis roles. | Resolved — deviated |\n    28\t| The epic requires the packaged skill to include the CLI, but the current build surface only composes markdown skill outputs and bundled references. | Feature overview, CLI responsibilities implied by scope | Add a build step that bundles the modular CLI source into a single Node-compatible JS file and copies that file into the packaged skill output. | Resolved — deviated |\n    29\t\n    30\t### Tech Design Question Coverage\n    31\t\n    32\tThe epic’s Tech Design Questions are answered by this pack as follows:\n    33\t\n    34\t| Question | Primary Answer Location |\n    35\t|---|---|\n    36\t| Public CLI commands, arguments, exit codes, result formats | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Command Surface, Result Contracts |\n    37\t| Prompt assets storage and assembly | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Prompt Asset System |\n    38\t| Three self-review passes | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\n    39\t| Model matrix and harness fallback | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Run Config + Provider Adapters |\n    40\t| Continuation identifiers | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Session Continuation Contracts |\n    41\t| Exact result fields and serialization | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Result Contracts |\n    42\t| `team-impl-log.md` state model | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Orchestrator State and Durable Artifacts |\n    43\t| How orchestrator consumes CLI results | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Process Playbook and Stage Transitions |\n    44\t| Verification-gate hardening | `tech-design.md` — Verification Scripts; `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Initialization Flow |\n    45\t| Companion tech-design naming convention | This index — document structure and companion naming |\n    46\t| Implementor replacement vs continuation | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Continue Rules |\n    47\t| Quick-fix narrow contract | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Quick Fix Workflow |\n    48\t| Pre-epic cleanup artifacts | `tech-design-skill-process.md` and `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Epic Cleanup |\n    49\t| Self-review prompt evolution and stop rules | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\n    50\t\n    51\t## Context\n    52\t\n    53\t`liminal-spec` is primarily a content-and-build repository, not a general application runtime. Most skills in the repo are markdown-first artifacts composed from `src/` and packaged into installable skill folders or standalone markdown files. That matters because `ls-claude-impl` is not a normal addition to the pack. It introduces a second kind of deliverable inside the same repo: a bundled runtime helper that must ship inside the packaged skill while still fitting the repository’s content-composition workflow.\n    54\t\n    55\tThe problem being solved is reliability of implementation orchestration, not lack of methodology. Existing implementation skill surfaces carry useful process ideas, but they rely on too much live runtime improvisation and too many layers between the orchestrator and the actual work. The new design narrows the system to three stable public surfaces. First, the skill teaches the orchestrator the process and the reading journey. Second, the orchestrator owns state and decision-making. Third, the CLI executes bounded operations and returns disciplined machine-readable results. The center of gravity shifts away from “agent teams all the way down” and toward “one orchestrator with explicit process docs and a legible execution surface.”\n    56\t\n    57\tThe future direction also matters now. The CLI is not just a local helper for this skill. It is an early public execution surface that is expected to map cleanly into a later SDK and then into web-app harness tools. That creates strong pressure toward explicit inputs, outputs, and outcome states. Hidden orchestration state inside the CLI would make later extraction harder. For the same reason, the skill should own the explanation of the process while the CLI owns execution. The orchestrator needs to understand what is happening at the process level without carrying implementation detail that belongs inside the runtime.\n    58\t\n    59\tA second pressure comes from prompt architecture. This feature does not only need CLI commands; it needs stable role prompts, reusable snippets, public insert hooks, and role-fit reading journeys. Those prompt assets are implementation-critical. They are not just docs, and they are not just helper strings. They are part of the runtime contract. The design therefore treats the prompt system as a first-class architectural surface rather than as glue hidden inside an implementation file.\n    60\t\n    61\tFinally, the repo’s existing build pipeline constrains how the feature lands. The current build emits markdown skill directories plus standalone markdown packs. It does not yet package executable runtime files into skill outputs. The design therefore includes build-system changes as part of the feature instead of leaving them to Story 0 discovery. The packaged skill must contain everything the orchestrator needs at runtime: markdown guidance plus a single bundled Node-compatible CLI file.\n    62\t\n    63\t## System View\n    64\t\n    65\tThere is no separate technical architecture document for this work. The top-tier surfaces below are therefore locally derived from the epic, nearby design notes, the existing `codex-impl` runtime, and the current repository layout.\n    66\t\n    67\t### Top-Tier Surfaces\n    68\t\n    69\t| Surface | Source | This Epic's Role |\n    70\t|---|---|---|\n    71\t| Skill Surface | Locally derived | `ls-claude-impl` markdown entrypoint and progressive-disclosure references that onboard the orchestrator and teach the process |\n    72\t| Runtime Surface | Locally derived | `ls-impl-cli` command surface, result contracts, provider adapters, prompt assembly, and packaging |\n    73\t| Build Surface | Locally derived | Manifest/build integration that composes the skill and embeds the bundled CLI artifact into packaged outputs |\n    74\t\n    75\t### System Context Diagram\n    76\t\n    77\t```mermaid\n    78\tflowchart LR\n    79\t    User[Human user] --> CC[Claude Code orchestrator]\n    80\t    CC --> Skill[ls-claude-impl SKILL.md]\n    81\t    Skill --> RefA[reading-journey.md]\n    82\t    Skill --> RefB[process-playbook.md]\n    83\t    Skill --> RefC[cli-operations.md]\n    84\t    Skill --> RefD[prompt-system.md]\n    85\t\n    86\t    CC --> SpecPack[Spec pack root]\n    87\t    SpecPack --> Epic[epic.md]\n    88\t    SpecPack --> TD[tech-design*.md]\n    89\t    SpecPack --> TP[test-plan.md]\n    90\t    SpecPack --> Stories[stories/]\n    91\t    SpecPack --> RunConfig[impl-run.config.json]\n    92\t    SpecPack --> Log[team-impl-log.md]\n    93\t\n    94\t    CC --> CLI[ls-impl-cli]\n    95\t    CLI --> PromptAssets[Embedded base prompts + snippets]\n    96\t    CLI --> Codex[Codex CLI]\n    97\t    CLI --> Copilot[Copilot CLI]\n    98\t    CLI --> Claude[Claude Code CLI]\n    99\t\n   100\t    CLI --> Results[Structured JSON results]\n   101\t    Results --> CC\n   102\t    CC --> Log\n   103\t```\n   104\t\n   105\t### External Contracts\n   106\t\n   107\tThe external contracts are narrower than the whole feature. They are the fixed points the orchestrator and CLI must agree on before implementation begins.\n   108\t\n   109\t#### Incoming Contracts\n   110\t\n   111\t| Input | Required | Source | Purpose |\n `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |\n   142\t\n   143\t#### Runtime Prerequisites\n   144\t\n   145\t| Prerequisite | Where Needed | How to Verify |\n   146\t|---|---|---|\n   147\t|
