  320\t\n   321\tThese tests are important even though they are not 1:1 with epic TCs.\n   322\t\n   323\t| Test | Why It Matters |\n   324\t|---|---|\n   325\t| invalid config rejects duplicate epic verifier labels | protects log/report clarity |\n   326\t| `story-continue` rejects mismatched story/session handle | prevents continuation drift |\n   327\t| provider adapters do not use “latest session by cwd” fallback | avoids accidental resume of the wrong session |\n   328\t| prompt assets are embedded into the generated runtime bundle | protects packaged-skill portability |\n   329\t| build output includes `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs` in packaged skill | protects distribution correctness |\n   330\t| packaged CLI `--help` works under Node after bundling | protects Node-compatible distribution target |\n   331\t| malformed provider output returns machine-readable CLI error | protects the provider parsing boundary |\n   332\t| ambiguous story ordering returns `needs-user-decision` | protects deterministic progression |\n   333\t| oversized prompt insert is rejected | protects prompt-surface trust boundary |\n   334\t| preflight rejects mismatched story/session continuation input | protects recovery integrity |\n   335\t| spec-pack root outside git repo is rejected | protects root trust boundary |\n   336\t| provider timeout maps to stable blocked/error result | protects subprocess reliability |\n   337\t| persisted stdout/stderr artifacts redact obvious secrets | protects durable artifact safety |\n   338\t| unreadable prompt insert returns `PROMPT_INSERT_INVALID` | protects insert-path handling |\n   339\t| log-template validation stays aligned with parser assumptions | protects orchestrator recovery surface |\n   340\t\n   341\t## Manual Verification Checklist\n   342\t\n   343\tAfter TDD Green for the final chunk, run a manual smoke on a fixture spec pack:\n   344\t\n   345\t1. Build the skill pack.\n   346\t2. Confirm the packaged skill contains:\n   347\t   - `SKILL.md`\n   348\t   - all four reference files\n   349\t   - `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs`\n   350\t3. Run:\n   351\t   - `node dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs --help`\n   352\t   - `node dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root <fixture>`\n   353\t   - `node dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs preflight --spec-pack-root <fixture>`\n   354\t4. docs/spec-build/epics/01-claude-impl-cli-skill/artifacts/story-00/001-implementor.jsonl:54:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_32","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' src/phases/claude-impl.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Team Implementation Orchestration — External CLI\n\n**Purpose:** Orchestrate story-by-story implementation using agent teams with an external CLI model (Codex or Copilot). You are the team lead — you spawn Opus teammates who manage external model subagents, route verification, make judgment calls, and move work forward.\n\nYou receive technically enriched stories (and optionally an epic and tech design). You implement them sequentially, one story at a time. Teammates manage the external CLI; you manage the process.\n\n---\n\n## Skill Reload Requirement\n\nReload this skill before starting each new story. This is a hard requirement, not a suggestion.\n\nIn repeated orchestration runs, models that skip this step exhibit progressive drift: control contract violations, orchestrator role creep into implementation work, verification shortcuts, and rationalized process skips. These failures compound across stories — the later stories in a sequence are the most vulnerable. In multiple production runs, drift that began around story 3-4 resulted in entire epics being rolled back and restarted from scratch.\n\nThe user will interrupt and roll back any story that was started without a fresh skill load.\n\nOn Load (below) detects whether this is the first load or a mid-epic reload and adjusts accordingly. The reload is lightweight — it refreshes directives and picks up where the log says to continue.\n\n---\n\n## The Orchestrator's Role\n\nYou are not the implementer. You are not the reviewer. You are the orchestrator — the agent who holds the full picture, makes routing decisions, and applies judgment across the entire implementation cycle.\n\nYour job:\n- Read and understand the stories, epic, and tech design before starting\n- Spawn a teammate for each story's implementation\n- Give clear, complete handoff instructions\n- Receive consolidated reports and decide what happens next\n- Track cumulative state across stories (test counts, regressions, patterns)\n- Escalate to the human only when you genuinely can't resolve something\n\nYou should be able to handle routine decisions autonomously — fix routing, severity assessment, pushback evaluation, loop termination. The human is the final authority on judgment calls you aren't confident about, not a checkpoint for every decision.\n\n**The orchestrator NEVER creates, modifies, or deletes code files.** This is absolute. In every orchestration run to date where the orchestrator touched code, it burned context needed for later stories, introduced completion bias (reviewing your own work), and conflated roles. The correct routing:\n\n- Quick fixes (typos, one-file adjustments) → fire a `senior-engineer` subagent\n- More extensive work (multi-file changes, architectural adjustments) → spawn a new general-purpose teammate\n- Verification gate → run the command yourself (this is the ONE implementation-adjacent action you take — it's a pass/fail check, not investigation)\n- If a test fails during the gate check → route it to a teammate or subagent. Do NOT debug it.\n\n### Autonomy and Forward Progress\n\nThe goal of this orchestration is to complete the work fully, with quality, and with maximal reasonable autonomy. Default to forward progress. When the next step is routine and clearly in service of the approved objective, take it — don't stop to ask permission.\n\nStop only when a genuine blocker requires human judgment. Examples of genuine blockers:\n\n- The external CLI is unavailable after investigation and retry\n- Verification reveals a meaningful correctness, security, or integration problem with unclear disposition\n- The spec or tech design appears inconsistent or insufficient in a way that affects implementation direction\n- A fix would materially expand scope or alter user-facing behavior\n- Project gates are failing for reasons that require a product or architectural decision, not a routine implementation fix\n\nEverything else — routine story transitions, clean gate passes, trivial fixes clearly within scope, mechanical cleanup — keep moving.\n\n---\n\n## On Load\n\n### Determine State\n\nCheck for an existing `team-impl-log.md` alongside the epic and tech design artifacts (or in the working directory). The log carries an explicit state field:\n\n- **No log exists** → this is a `SETUP` (first load). Execute full initialization below.\n- **Log exists with state `BETWEEN_STORIES`** → mid-epic reload. Read the log for current position, cumulative test count, selected CLI, and recorded patterns. The directive refresh happened when the skill loaded. Proceed to the next story.\n- **Log exists with state `STORY_ACTIVE`** → reload during an active story (error recovery). Read the log for which story is in progress. Resume the current story cycle.\n- **Log exists with state `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY`** → all stories accepted. Proceed to the Epic-Level Verification section below.\n\n### Full Initialization (SETUP only)\n\n#### 1. Initialize the Log\n\nCreate `team-impl-log.md` alongside the epic and tech design artifacts. Set state to `SETUP`. This log captures the full orchestration experience — CLI selection, verification gates, handoff templates, story-level observations, and process adaptations. It is a first-class deliverable. A perfect implementation with thin notes is less valuable than a messy implementation with thorough orchestration documentation.\n\n#### 2. Select External CLI\n\nAsk the user which external CLI to use: **Codex** or **Copilot**. Record their choice in the log.\n\nThen load the selected skill with the Skill tool:\n- Codex → `Skill(codex-subagent)`\n- Copilot → `Skill(copilot-subagent)`\n\nIf the skill load fails, report the exact error. Do not proceed without a working external CLI. There is no fallback mode — this skill requires an external model for verification. Tell the user and stop.\n\nVerify the CLI works by running a simple test command. Record the result in the log.\n\nModel configuration: use `gpt-5.4` for all implementation and verification passes.\n\n#### 3. Verification Gate Discovery\n\nBefore Story 1 starts, discover and lock the project's verification gates.\n\n1. Read project policy docs (for example: `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `README`, package scripts, CI config) to identify required verification commands and development methodology (TDD, etc.).\n2. Define and log two gate sets in `team-impl-log.md`:\n   - **Story acceptance gate**: commands required before accepting an individual story\n   - **Epic acceptance gate**: commands required before final epic acceptance\n3. If policy is ambiguous, ask the human once before implementation begins.\n\nDo not assume unit tests alone are sufficient. Use the project's complete gate (including integration/e2e when required by project policy).\n\n#### 4. Collect Artifacts\n\nAsk the human what artifacts are available:\n- **Story** (required) — at minimum one technically enriched story to implement\n- **Epic** (optional) — the full feature specification the stories derive from\n- **Tech design** (optional) — the architecture and interface definitions\n\nEpic and tech design are optional because stories may exist without a separate epic or tech design document. The story is always required.\n\n**Implementation prompt discovery:** check whether per-story implementation prompts exist alongside the stories. If a `prompts/` directory (or similar) contains `story-N-implementation-prompt.md` files, these should be treated as the primary CLI launch input. Log their presence and paths. Teammates should pipe these directly to the CLI rather than writing prompts from scratch.\n\nOnce the human provides paths, read the epic and tech design if available. List the stories and read the first story. If there are multiple stories, read the first two to understand the dependency relationship and scope progression.\n\nUnderstanding the cross-cutting patterns in the tech design is the most important preparation. The stories contain the what (ACs, TCs). The tech design contains the architecture context that ties everything together — how modules interact, why decisions were made, what the flow patterns are. An orchestrator who only reads stories will miss the connections that inform good handoff decisions.\n\nNote the story sequence and dependency chain. Identify which stories are foundational (types, config — usually Story 0), which are the core stories, and which are integration/polish stories.\n\n#### 5. Boundary Inventory\n\nIdentify all external service dependencies from the epic and tech design. Create an inventory in the log:\n\n| Boundary | Status | Story |\n|----------|--------|-------|\n| Auth API | stub | 0 |\n| Payment gateway | not started | 3 |\n\nTrack status: `stub` / `mocked` / `integrated`. Check at every story transition. If any external boundary is still a stub at epic verification, flag it as P0 regardless of what previous reviewers said about it.\n\nIn a previous run, the primary external integration remained a throwing stub across 3 epics, 26 reviews, and 302 tests. Mock-first testing tested the mocks, not the integration. The boundary inventory exists to prevent this.\n\n#### 6. Materialize Handoff Templates\n\nWrite the implementer and reviewer handoff templates to `team-impl-log.md`. These templates are the source of truth for every teammate dispatch. Re-read them before constructing each handoff prompt.\n\nThis converts a recall task (remembering the skill's instructions) into a reference task (reading a file). In a previous run, the orchestrator loaded the skill, executed On Load correctly, then forgot the core process by story 3. The handoff templates survived because they were in the log.\n\n**Implementer template:**\n\n```\nYou are implementing a story. You are a supervisory layer over a CLI subagent. You do NOT implement directly.\n\nStep 1 — Load skill:\n  Use the Skill tool: Skill({CLI_SKILL_NAME})\n  If this fails, report the exact error. Do not skip it. Do not implement yourself.\n\nStep 2 — Read artifacts sequentially, reflecting after each one:\n  Read each file one at a time. After reading each file, stop and reflect on what you\n  learned before reading the next. Write your reflections as you go — these become\n  compressed context that persists with strong attention weight throughout your work.\n\n  1. [cross-cutting decisions / tech design index] — Read. Reflect: what are the key\n     architectural decisions, vocabulary, and cross-cutting patterns?\n  2. [primary tech design companion for this story] — Read. Reflect: what interfaces,\n     data contracts, and design constraints are relevant?\n  3. [epic] — Read. Reflect: how does this story fit in the broader feature? What are\n     the upstream/downstream dependencies?\n  4. [test plan if available] — Read. Reflect: what testing patterns and coverage\n     expectations apply?\n  5. [story] — Read. Reflect: what are the ACs, TCs, and any spec deviations or\n     gotchas to flag?\n\n  Write your cumulative reflections to /tmp/reflection-story-N.md before touching code.\n\nStep 3 — Write a CLI prompt and launch:\n  [If implementation prompts exist: pipe the prompt directly to CLI]\n  [Otherwise: write a lean, execution-oriented prompt with artifact paths]\n  Give the CLI the same sequential reading and per-file reflection instructions.\n  Use gpt-5.4. Launch async. Wait for completion.\n\nStep 6 — Self-review loop:\n  Tell the CLI to do a thorough critical self-review. Fix non-controversial issues.\n  If substantive changes, iterate. As more stories are accepted and committed, the orchestrator builds up investment in forward progress. This creates pressure to rubber-stamp reviews, downplay findings, skip verification steps, and quick-fix things personally. The later stories in a sequence are more vulnerable than the earlier ones. The invariants are external constraints, not suggestions to evaluate against your confidence level.\\n\\n### Adaptive Controls\\n\\nKeep process flexibility without weakening the invariants:\\n\\n- Risk-tier each story (`low`, `medium`, `high`) and scale verification depth accordingly.\\n- Use bounded review loops; avoid churn when no substantive changes remain.\\n- Use `accepted-risk` for explicitly reasoned, non-blocking issues.\\n- Human override always takes precedence over default routing.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## External Model Failure Protocol\\n\\nWhen a teammate reports that the CLI is unavailable or didn't work:\\n\\n1. First report → tell them to try again.\\n2. Second report → tell them to try again.\\n3. Third report → test the CLI yourself with a simple command.\\n4. If CLI works → send the teammate back with proof that the CLI is operational.\\n5. If CLI is genuinely down → escalate to the human.\\n\\nIf a teammate did verification itself instead of using the CLI — i.e., they report findings but have no CLI session ID — shut down that teammate immediately. Spawn a fresh teammate with the same instructions. Do not accept the work the previous teammate did. The verification is not complete.\\n\\nThe explanation for why the CLI didn't work will sound plausible. In multiple runs, teammates reported \\\"Codex CLI not available in environment\\\" while other teammates on the same machine were using it successfully. The teammate read the skill wrong, hit a transient error, or confabulated a reason to skip. Do not accept it.\\n\\nRuns where external model verification was skipped have consistently resulted in undetected bugs — including security vulnerabilities, spec deviations, and integration gaps — that were later caught by retroactive verification passes. The user will likely roll back work that was done without proper verification.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Story Implementation Cycle\\n\\nFor each story in sequence:\\n\\n### 1. Spawn the Implementer\\n\\nSet log state to `STORY_ACTIVE` and record which story is being implemented and the current phase (`implementing` or `reviewing`). Update the phase when transitioning to verification. This ensures a mid-story reload can resume at the correct point in the cycle.\\n\\nSpawn a general-purpose teammate (Opus, not senior-engineer). The teammate is the supervisory layer — it manages a CLI subagent, verifies the output, and reports back to you.\\n\\nRe-read the materialized implementer template from the log. Construct the handoff as one complete prompt (not drip-fed). Substitute the actual paths and any story-specific flags you noticed while reading — like \\\"this story has a spec deviation worth noting\\\" or \\\"this is a large story, ~40 tests expected.\\\"\\n\\nIf per-story implementation prompts exist, tell the teammate to use them as the primary CLI input.\\n\\n### 2. Verification\\n\\nWhen the implementer reports back, the implementation has already been through one or more rounds of self-review. The easy issues are fixed. What remains is either clean or genuinely ambiguous. Now it gets a fresh set of eyes.\\n\\n**Spawn the reviewer.** Update the log phase to `reviewing`. A fresh general-purpose Opus teammate (not senior-engineer). Re-read the materialized reviewer template from the log. Same artifacts, explicit CLI requirement.\\n\\nThe reviewer runs a dual review: CLI spec-compliance check in parallel with the reviewer's own architectural review. Two perspectives — the external model's literal compliance check and the Opus reviewer's architectural judgment.\\n\\nWhen the CLI review comes back, the Opus reviewer consolidates both sets of findings, verifies claims against the actual code, compiles fixes, launches CLI to implement fixes, and iterates until clean.\\n\\nOn first review round, ask: \\\"What else did you notice but did not report?\\\" Capture additional observations before final synthesis.\\n\\n### 3. Orchestrator Final Check\\n\\nWhen the reviewer reports back, the implementation has been through multiple review layers: CLI build → iterated self-review → fresh Opus + CLI dual review → fixes → iterated self-review. Two separate agents and two separate CLI instances have looked at it.\\n\\n1. **Run the discovered story acceptance gate yourself** — execute the exact commands you locked in Verification Gate Discovery and confirm they pass. Don't trust reports alone.\\n2. **Review the report's open issues** — if either teammate surfaced issues they didn't fix, assess them from the report. Route any remaining fixes to a senior-engineer subagent (quick) or fresh teammate (extensive).\\n3. **Do NOT read implementation files yourself.** Do NOT debug failures. Route them.\\n\\n**Accepting the story:**\\n\\nBefore acceptance, write a pre-acceptance receipt to the log:\\n\\n1. CLI evidence reference (session ID(s) and/or output artifact reference)\\n2. Top findings and dispositions (`fixed`, `accepted-risk`, `defer`)\\n3. Exact story gate command(s) run and result summary\\n4. Open risks (or `none`)\\n\\nOnce satisfied — all gates pass, no open issues, code looks right — stage all changes and commit: `feat: Story N — [story title]`. Each story gets its own commit. Don't amend previous commits.\\n\\n### 4. Story Transition\\n\\nWhen a story is accepted and committed:\\n\\n1. Write a transition checkpoint to `team-impl-log.md` as the final action of the completed story cycle. Include: problems encountered, impact, resolution, recommendations, pre-acceptance receipt fields, cumulative test count.\\n2. Update boundary inventory — check status of all external dependencies.\\n3. Update log state:\\n   - If more stories remain → set state to `BETWEEN_STORIES`\\n   - If this was the last story in the epic → set state to `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY`\\n\\n**If more stories remain: RELOAD THIS SKILL and continue.** If the next story was started without reloading the skill, the user will interrupt and roll back.\\n\\n**If this was the last story of a multi-story epic:** proceed to Pre-Verification Cleanup, then Epic-Level Verification.\\n\\n**Fresh agents per story.** Every story gets a fresh implementer and a fresh reviewer. No carrying forward teammates between stories. The new teammate reads the story cold with no assumptions from previous work. If the story spec isn't sufficient for cold implementation, that's a spec gap to flag, not a reason to carry context forward.\\n\\n**Don't tear down the active teammate path until the replacement is confirmed.** Spawn the next teammate before shutting down the current one.\\n\\n**Track cumulative test counts explicitly.** After each story, record the total test count. Before kicking off the next story, note the expected baseline: \\\"Story 2 ended at 43 tests. Story 3's TC mapping specifies ~12 tests. After Story 3, total should be approximately 55.\\\" If the total after the next story is less than the previous total, something regressed — investigate before accepting.\\n\\n**Regression is a stop-the-line event.** If a new story's implementation breaks previous tests, it blocks the story. The orchestrator's final check should verify the full test suite, not just the new story's tests.\\n\\n**What carries forward between stories:**\\n- The committed codebase — each story builds on the previous\\n- Cumulative test count and verification baseline\\n- Patterns the orchestrator noticed — if Story 2's CLI drifted on error formats, flag that in Story 3's handoff\\n- `team-impl-log.md` — each story's orchestration experience informs the next\\n- Boundary inventory\\n\\n**What doesn't carry forward:**\\n- Teammates — fresh per story\\n- Assumptions about what previous implementers \\\"know\\\" — each agent starts cold\\n- Unresolved issues from previous stories — if it wasn't fixed and committed, it doesn't exist for the next story's agent\\n\\n### 5. Escalation Handling\\n\\nWhen teammates escalate issues or problems arise during any phase:\\n\\n1. **Assess the situation yourself.** Read whatever context you need from the report. Don't just forward the question.\\n2. **Reflect against the epic and tech design.** The artifacts contain the rationale for decisions. Most questions can be answered by tracing back to the spec.\\n3. **If you can make a reasonable decision, make it.** Route the answer back to the teammate with your reasoning.\\n4. **If you need the human's ruling:** explain what's needed, what you did to investigate, what you understand about the issue, your recommendation, and your reasoning. Give the human enough context to decide without re-investigating from scratch.\\n\\nIf the human interrupts with process feedback (not task-level content feedback), enter `PAUSED_PROCESS_REVIEW` mode immediately:\\n- Stop new dispatches.\\n- Do not commit.\\n- Diagnose the process behavior with the human.\\n- Resume only after explicit human instruction to resume orchestration.\\n\\nIn `PAUSED_PROCESS_REVIEW`, prioritize diagnosis over reassurance. Treat frustration as diagnostic signal.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Pre-Verification Cleanup\\n\\nAfter all stories are accepted and before epic-level verification, compile all deferred and accepted-risk items from every story cycle into a single list.\\n\\nPresent the categorized list to the human — this is a batch with mixed severity and is worth the human seeing before dispatch. Include small items. Do not defer trivial items just because they're small — if the fix is a few lines, it is faster to fix than to track.\\n\\n**Materialize the complete fix list to a file before constructing any handoff prompt.** When context distance grows between when items are discussed and when the handoff is written, small items drop off. Write the numbered list to a file, read that file when writing the handoff, paste its contents into the prompt. This is a structural intervention — advisory \\\"remember to include everything\\\" does not reliably work.\\n\\nBundle the approved items into one fix batch. Implement as a normal story cycle: implementer teammate with CLI, reviewer teammate with CLI, orchestrator final check. Commit as `fix: pre-verification cleanup`.\\n\\nThen proceed to epic-level verification with a cleaner codebase.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Epic-Level Verification\\n\\nAfter all stories are accepted and committed (and pre-verification cleanup is done), run a full-codebase review before shipping. This also applies to a single-story implementation.\\n\\n**RELOAD THIS SKILL before starting epic verification.**\\n\\nThis is not optional. Do not ask the user whether to do it. In previous runs, undetected integration gaps, stub implementations shipped as production code, and contract inconsistencies survived 26 story-level reviews and were only caught by the epic-level verification pass.\\n\\n### Setup\\n\\nCreate a verification output directory with a subdirectory per reviewer. For example:\\n\\n```\\nverification/\\n  opus/\\n  sonnet/\\n  gpt54/\\n  gpt53-codex/\\n```\\n\\n### Phase 1: Four Parallel Reviews\\n\\nLaunch four reviewers simultaneously. Each reads the full epic, the full tech design, and the entire codebase. Each writes a detailed review report to their designated directory.\\n\\n**Two Claude teammates** (general-purpose, not senior-engineer):\\n\\n1. **Opus reviewer** — reads epic, tech design, all source files, all test files. Writes `epic-review.md` to their directory.\\n2. **Sonnet reviewer** — same artifacts, same task, writes to their directory.\\n\\n**Two external model reviews** (each managed by a general-purpose teammate who loads the CLI skill):\\n\\n3. **gpt-5.4 review** — the primary external verifier. Same artifacts, writes review. The teammate captures the output and writes the report file.\\n4. **gpt-5.3-codex review** — secondary diversity verifier, if available. Same artifacts, writes review. Same capture pattern. If gpt-5.3-codex is not available, proceed with three reviewers.\\n\\nEach reviewer's prompt:\\n\\n- Read the epic (path), the tech design (path), and every source and test file in the project\\n- Specify the exact working directory for CLI reviewers\\n- Do a thorough critical review of the full implementation against the epic and tech design\\n- Organize findings by severity (Critical, Major, Minor)\\n- Verify AC/TC coverage, interface compliance, architecture alignment, test quality\\n- Check boundary inventory: is any external dependency still a stub?\\n- Write the full report to their output file\\n\\n**Wait for all reports before proceeding.** Do not start Phase 2 until every report is written.\\n\\n### Phase 2: Meta-Reports\\n\\nSend each reviewer the paths to all review reports. Each reviewer reads all reports and writes a meta-report to their directory:\\n\\n- Rank the reports from best to worst\\n- For each report: what's good about it, what's not good about it\\n- Describe what they would take from each report if synthesizing a single best review\\n\\n**Wait for all meta-reports before proceeding.**\\n\\n### Phase 3: Orchestrator Synthesis\\n\\nRead all review reports and all meta-reports. Produce a synthesized assessment:\\n\\n1. **Cross-reference findings.** Build a table: which findings appear in multiple reports (high confidence), which are unique to one reviewer (investigate).\\n2. **Assess severity.** Claude models tend to grade generously. External models tend to grade conservatively. Apply your own judgment — don't average.\\n3. **Categorize the fix list:**\\n   - Must-fix: ship blockers\\n   - Should-fix: correctness or quality issues\\n   - Trivial: small fixes that take a few lines of code\\n4. **Present the categorized fix list to the human** with your recommended ship-readiness grade. This is a large batch with mixed severity — the human should see it before you dispatch.\\n5. **Materialize the complete fix list to a file** before constructing the handoff prompt. Include all items the human approved — must-fix, should-fix, and trivial. Do not filter out small items. Context distance causes drops; the list must exist as a readable artifact.\\n\\n### Phase 4: Fixes\\n\\nAfter discussion and human approval:\\n\\n- Launch a teammate with the CLI to implement the approved fixes. Give them the fix list file, the epic, and the tech design.\\n- After fixes: launch a fresh review targeting the specific changes to confirm correctness.\\n- Run the discovered epic acceptance gate yourself to confirm all required gates pass.\\n- Check boundary inventory one final time: no external dependencies should remain as stubs.\\n- Stage, commit (`fix: epic verification fixes`), and report completion to the human.\\n\\nUpdate log state to `COMPLETE`.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Operational Patterns\\n\\nThese patterns emerged from real orchestration experience and encode failure modes the skill needs to handle.\\n\\n### Idle Notifications Are Unreliable Signals\\n\\nTeammates emit idle notifications between turns. These are noise during multi-step tasks — a teammate doing a 15-minute implementation will fire multiple idle notifications while actively working. Do not interpret idle notifications as \\\"the agent is done\\\" or \\\"the agent is stuck.\\\"\\n\\nThe reliable signal is the teammate's explicit message reporting results. Wait for that. If extended time passes with no message (calibrate based on task complexity), send a brief nudge: \\\"Did you complete the work? Report your results.\\\" Don't assume failure from silence alone.\\n\\n### Context Ceilings\\n\\nAgents that read the full epic + tech design + story, implement multiple modules, and then process review feedback can exhaust their context window. Symptoms: the agent goes idle without completing, or produces truncated/confused output.\\n\\nMitigation: the human configures model context size. If an agent hits context limits, the human may need to intervene to adjust model settings. The orchestrator cannot control context size at spawn time — flag the issue and let the human handle it.\\n\\n### Agents Forget to Report Back\\n\\nAfter long multi-step tasks (15+ minutes, dozens of tool calls), agents sometimes complete their work but forget to send the completion message back to the team lead. The \\\"report back to team lead\\\" instruction decays over a long execution chain.\\n\\nThis is structural, not random — longer tasks make it more likely. The handoff prompt should place the reporting instruction prominently. If two idle notifications pass after expected completion time with no message, send a nudge.\\n\\n### Sequencing: Wait for Confirmation Before Proceeding\\n\\nDo not launch the next phase of work until the current agent confirms completion. Specifically:\\n- Don't launch verification before the implementer signals \\\"done\\\"\\n- Don't launch the next story before the current story is fully verified\\n- Don't assume file state is final because you can read correct-looking files — the agent may have more changes in flight\\n\\nThe teammate's explicit report is the trigger for the next step, not the orchestrator's independent observation of file state.\\n\\n### Completion Bias Degrades the Orchestrator\\n\\nAs more stories are accepted, the orchestrator builds up investment in forward progress. This creates pressure to rubber-stamp reviews, downplay findings, skip verification steps, and quick-fix things personally. This is structural, not a character flaw. The later stories are more vulnerable.\\n\\nThe hard invariants and the skill reload requirement exist precisely for this reason. They are external constraints, not suggestions to evaluate.\\n\\n### Context Stripping Is Expected\\n\\nFor runs over 5 stories, context may need to be stripped (tool calls removed by the human) to free space. This is a normal operational event, not an emergency. The orchestration log survives context stripping. The session can continue. The important state is in the log and the committed codebase, not in the orchestrator's context.\\n\\n### Item List Drops During Dispatch\\n\\nWhen context distance grows between when items are discussed and when a handoff prompt is written, small items fall off. The orchestrator interprets \\\"the list\\\" as \\\"the things we just discussed in detail\\\" rather than \\\"all remaining items.\\\" Materialize the complete list to a file before writing any handoff prompt. Read the file when constructing the prompt.\\n\\n### CLI Reviewed Wrong Target\\n\\nIn a previous run, a CLI subagent reviewed an old prototype directory instead of the current application directory. The handoff prompt must specify the exact working directory. Verify in the teammate's report that the CLI ran against the correct path.\\n\\n### Large Fix Batches Need Human Eyes\\n\\nIn a previous run, an orchestrator auto-dispatched all 16 epic-verification fixes without presenting them first. Three items changed disposition through discussion — including one that would have been a scope change. When you have a batch of fixes with mixed severity and disposition, present the categorized list and let the human weigh in before dispatching. This applies to epic verification synthesis and pre-verification cleanup — situations where you're routing a significant volume of fixes at once. It does not mean every routine decision needs human approval.\\n\\n### Process Adaptation\\n\\nThe workflow defined above is the default. The orchestrator has discretion to adjust within bounds.\\n\\nWhat can be adjusted:\\n- How much detail goes into the handoff prompt based on story complexity\\n- Whether to flag specific risks or gotchas based on patterns from previous stories\\n- Risk-tier verification depth\\n- Bounded review loop depth when no substantive issues remain\\n- Use of explicit `accepted-risk` dispositions for non-blocking items\\n\\nWhat cannot be adjusted:\\n- The self-review loop always runs\\n- The orchestrator always runs the discovered story/epic acceptance gates\\n- Fresh agents per story\\n- Full test suite regression check\\n- The three hard invariants in Control Contract\\n- Skill reload before each story\\n- External model verification — no substituting your own review\\n\\nHuman override always wins. If the human directs a process change, apply it, log it, and continue.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Logging\\n\\n`team-impl-log.md` is created during On Load and lives alongside the epic and tech design. It captures the full orchestration experience for this run.\\n\\n**Mandatory fields:**\\n- `state`: one of `SETUP`, `BETWEEN_STORIES`, `STORY_ACTIVE`, `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY`, `COMPLETE`\\n- `cli`: selected CLI skill name\\n- Verification gates (story and epic)\\n- Materialized handoff templates\\n- Boundary inventory\\n- Story sequence with dependency chain\\n\\n**What to log per story:**\\n- Transition checkpoints with pre-acceptance receipt fields\\n- Decisions and their reasoning\\n- Corrections the human makes to your process\\n- Failure modes encountered and how they were resolved\\n- Patterns that emerge across stories\\n- Process evolution (when and why the workflow adapted)\\n\\n**What not to log:**\\n- Status updates (\\\"Story 3 started\\\")\\n- Routine events that went as expected\\n- Implementation details (that's the code's job)\\n\\nWrite narrative entries, not bullet points. Each entry should tell the story of what happened, what was observed, and why it might matter. A future reader building or refining this skill needs to understand the shape of the work, not just the checkboxes.\\n\"\n\n      at <anonymous> helper, skill/reference docs, and bundled-artifact build plumbing.  \n- Likely failure modes: invalid spec-pack layout detection, four-file companion recognition, stale `claude-impl` skill content, temp-dist bundling, and bundled runtime portability under plain Node.\n\n**changed files**  \n- `processes/impl-cli/cli.ts` (new citty entrypoint for `ls-impl-cli`), `processes/impl-cli/core/fs-utils.ts` (Node-safe filesystem helpers for bundled runtime).  \n- `processes/impl-cli/tests/test-helpers.ts` (temp spec-pack + CLI helpers), `scripts/__tests__/claude-impl-build.test.ts` (skill registration, references, bundled artifact coverage).  \n- `src/phases/claude-impl.md` (full overwrite with correct `ls-claude-impl` startup content), `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md`, `src/references/claude-impl-process-playbook.md`, `src/references/claude-impl-cli-operations.md`, `src/references/claude-impl-prompt-system.md` (new progressive-disclosure references).  \n- `manifest.json` (register `ls-claude-impl` + bundled artifact metadata), `scripts/build.ts` (bundle/copy `bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs` and honor `DIST_DIR`), `scripts/validate.ts` (validate bundled artifacts), `src/README-pack.md`, `src/README-markdown-pack.md` (list the new skill/standalone output).  \n- `package.json` (add `typecheck`, `red-verify`, `green-verify`, `verify-all`, `guard:no-test-changes`, `smoke:impl-cli`, `capture:test-baseline`, plus `node >=22` engine), `tsconfig.json` (typecheck config for scripts + `impl-cli`).\n\n**tests: added, modified, removed; total count after story; delta vs prior baseline (prior = 57)**  \n- Added: `processes/impl-cli/tests/inspect-command.test.ts`, `processes/impl-cli/tests/log-template-contract.test.ts`, `scripts/__tests__/claude-impl-skill.test.ts`, `scripts/__tests__/claude-impl-build.test.ts`.  \n- Modified: none.  \n- Removed: none.  \n- Total count after story: 78.  \n- Delta vs prior baseline: +21.\n\n**gates run + result (pass / fail / not-run)**  \n- Targeted red suite for the new Story 0 tests: fail, expected.  \n- `bun run green-verify`: pass.  \n- `bun run smoke:impl-cli`: pass with visible skip notice, expected for Story 0.  \n- `node dist/skills/ls-claude-impl/bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs inspect --spec-pack-root .test-tmp/manual-node-smoke/spec-pack --json`: pass.  \n- Dependency usability check (`citty`, `c12`, `zod` imports): pass.\n\n**self-review findings fixed in this dispatch**  \n- Bundled build initially wrote artifact paths against the repo’s default `dist/` and broke temp-dist tests; `scripts/build.ts` now resolves bundled artifact sources against the active `DIST_DIR`.  \n- Bundled CLI initially failed under Node because `impl-cli` core used `Bun.*` filesystem APIs; runtime file IO now uses Node filesystem APIs.  \n- `cli.ts` initially used top-level `await`, which broke CommonJS bundling; entrypoint now uses `runMain(...).catch(...)`.\n\n**self-review findings surfaced to orchestrator (uncertain or unresolved)**  \n- `smoke:impl-cli` is intentionally still a visible skip in `processes/impl-cli/**/*.ts` instead of all `processes/**/*.ts`, to avoid pulling unrelated legacy `processes/codex-impl` type debt into the new Story 0 gate.\n\n**recommended next step**  \n- Start 312\t\n   313\t## Flow 2: Progressive Reading Journey\n   314\t\n   315\t**Covers:** AC-1.5, AC-3.4\n   316\t\n   317\tOnce the spec-pack root is known and the basic files are present, the orchestrator needs a deliberate reading path. The reading journey exists to prevent both under-reading and over-reading. If the orchestrator reads everything up front, it burns context and weakens later attention. If it reads too little, it makes routing decisions without understanding the feature.\n   318\t\n   319\tThe reading-journey reference should therefore do three things. It should define the read order. It should say what to extract into durable state after each read. And it should explicitly delay lower-value reads until they are needed. The orchestrator should come out of the journey with a stable mental model of the story queue, the tech-design shape, the verification gates, and the current run configuration.\n   320\t\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

The orchestrator should be able to inspect a spec pack before it has chosen or authored a run config, but it should not launch any provider-backed work until preflight has passed.\n   429\t\n   430\t```mermaid\n   431\tsequenceDiagram\n   432\t    participant Orchestrator\n   433\t    participant CLI\n   434\t    participant SpecPack\n   435\t    participant Config as impl-run.config.json\n   436\t    participant Providers as Provider CLIs\n   437\t\n   438\t    Note over Orchestrator,Providers: AC-1.1 to AC-2.4\n   439\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: inspect --spec-pack-root <path>\n   440\t    CLI->>SpecPack: Resolve required files, story order, insert presence\n   441\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Spec-pack summary\n   442\t    Orchestrator->>CLI: preflight --spec-pack-root <path>\n   443\t    CLI->>Config: Parse and validate run config\n   444\t    CLI->>Providers: Validate requested harness availability\n   445\t    CLI-->>Orchestrator: Readiness report + validated provider matrix\n   446\t```\n   447\t\n   448\t### Inspect Result Contract\n   449\t\n   450\t```typescript\n   451\texport interface InspectResult {\n   452\t  status: \"ready\" | \"needs-user-decision\" | \"blocked\";\n   453\t  specPackRoot: string;\n   454\t  techDesignShape: \"two-file\" | \"four-file\";\n   455\t  artifacts: {\n   456\t    epicPath: string;\n   457\t    techDesignPath: string;\n   458\t    techDesignCompanionPaths: string[];\n   459\t    testPlanPath: string;\n   460\t    storiesDir: string;\n   461\t  };\n   462\t  stories: Array<{ id: string; title: string; path: string; order: number }>;\n   463\t  inserts: {\n   464\t    customStoryImplPromptInsert: \"present\" | \"absent\";\n   465\t    customStoryVerifierPromptInsert: \"present\" | \"absent\";\n   466\t  };\n   467\t  blockers: string[];\n   468\t  notes: string[];\n   469\t}\n   470\t```\n   471\t\n   472\t### Preflight Result Contract\n   473\t\n   474\t```typescript\n   475\texport interface PreflightResult {\n   476\t  status: \"ready\" | \"needs-user-decision\" | \"blocked\";\n   477\t  validatedConfig: ImplRunConfig;\n   478\t  providerMatrix: ProviderMatrix;\n   479\t  verificationGates?: {\n   480\t    storyGate: string;\n   481\t    epicGate: string;\n   482\t    source: string;\n   483\t  };\n   484\t  configValidationNotes: string[];\n   485\t  promptAssets: {\n   486\t    basePromptsReady: boolean;\n   487\t    snippetsReady: boolean;\n   488\t    notes: string[];\n   489\t  };\n   490\t  blockers: string[];\n   491\t  notes: string[];\n   492\t}\n   493\t```\n   494\t\n   495\t`preflight` validates the authored config and reports the consequences of that config. It does **not** generate or rewrite the default matrix in v1. Default selection is part of the skill/process contract and is owned by the orchestrator before preflight runs.\n   496\t\n   497\t### Story Ordering and Story ID Rules\n   498\t\n   499\tSpec-pack resolution must not invent story order heuristically.\n   500\t\n   501\t1. Read markdown files from `stories/`, excluding `coverage.md`.\n   502\t2. Derive `storyId` from the filename without extension.\n   503\t3. Order stories by the leading numeric prefix in the filename when present.\n   504\t4. If two files have the same numeric prefix, fall back to lexical filename order.\n   505\t5. If no numeric prefixes exist anywhere, use lexical filename order and emit a warning in `inspect`.\n   506\t6. start. | `ready-for-verification`, `needs-followup-fix`, `needs-human-ruling`, `blocked` | Route verification or follow-up work based on the returned outcome. |\n    12\t| `story-continue` | The Purpose\\n     4\\t\\n     5\\tThis design translates the `ls-claude-impl` epic into an implementable architecture for a new Claude Code orchestration skill plus a Node-compatible implementation CLI. The feature has two dense domains that need different levels of treatment: the orchestrator-facing skill surface and the CLI/runtime surface that executes bounded operations for multiple secondary harnesses. For that reason this design uses **Config B**:\\n     6\\t\\n     7\\t| Document | Purpose |\\n     8\\t|---|---|\\n     9\\t| `tech-design.md` | Decision record, context, system view, top-tier surfaces, module architecture overview, dependency and packaging decisions, work breakdown |\\n    10\\t| `tech-design-skill-process.md` | The orchestrator-facing skill surface: progressive disclosure docs, reading journey, state ownership, process playbook, stage transitions |\\n    11\\t| `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` | The CLI/runtime surface: command model, config schemas, prompt asset system, provider adapters, operation contracts, packaging |\\n    12\\t| `test-plan.md` | TC-to-test mapping, mock strategy, fixture strategy, test counts, chunk-level TDD plan |\\n    13\\t\\n    14\\tThe index stays small enough to be navigable while still recording the cross-cutting decisions that both companion docs inherit. The companion split follows the real boundary in this feature: one side teaches the orchestrator how the process works, the other side executes the process through a clean agent-first CLI.\\n    15\\t\\n    16\\t## Spec Validation\\n    17\\t\\n    18\\tThe epic is designable. Its major flows, ACs, and TCs describe a coherent system with clear public seams: spec-pack discovery, run configuration, prompt assembly, retained implementation sessions, fresh verification, cleanup, and epic closeout. The epic is unusually detailed for a skill feature, but the added specificity is appropriate because the feature is not just a prose skill; it is a skill plus a runtime helper plus a prompt system plus a result-contract surface.\\n    19\\t\\n    20\\tThe design does make three clarifications that should be recorded explicitly before implementation begins. None of them block design, but each one turns an implied behavior into a concrete contract the implementation can build around.\\n    21\\t\\n    22\\t| Issue | Spec Location | Resolution | Status |\\n    23\\t|---|---|---|---|\\n    24\\t| The epic requires the orchestrator to record role defaults and run choices, but it does not name a dedicated machine-readable run config file. | AC-2.4, Data Contracts, `team-impl-log.md` requirements | Introduce `impl-run.config.json` at the spec-pack root as the orchestrator-owned run configuration. `team-impl-log.md` remains the durable narrative/state record. | Resolved — clarified |\\n    25\\t| The epic distinguishes Codex/Copilot-backed GPT lanes from Claude-only fallback, but the harness terminology needs to preserve Claude Code as the always-present primary harness. | AC-2.2, AC-2.3, Role Model Matrix | Use `primary_harness: \\\"claude-code\\\"` plus per-role `secondary_harness: \\\"codex\\\" | \\\"copilot\\\" | \\\"none\\\"`. `none` means the role uses the primary harness only. | Resolved — clarified |\\n    26\\t| The epic says the CLI should be stateless across calls, but story implementation also requires retained implementor continuity. | Constraint C3, AC-4.2, AC-6.3 | Keep orchestration state outside the CLI. The CLI may emit continuation/session handles for provider sessions, but the orchestrator owns persistence, routing, and recovery through `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any future orchestrator-managed support files. | Resolved — clarified |\\n    27\\t| The epic allows Copilot as the generic GPT fallback, but v1 Copilot support is limited to fresh-session roles while story implementation requires retained continuation. | AC-2.2, AC-4.2, Role Model Matrix | In v1, disallow Copilot for `story_implementor`. Retained implementor fallback order is Codex -> Claude Code primary harness. Copilot remains valid for fresh verifier, quick-fix, and synthesis roles. | Resolved — deviated |\\n    28\\t| The epic requires the packaged skill to include the CLI, but the current build surface only composes markdown skill outputs and bundled references. | Feature overview, CLI responsibilities implied by scope | Add a build step that bundles the modular CLI source into a single Node-compatible JS file and copies that file into the packaged skill output. | Resolved — deviated |\\n    29\\t\\n    30\\t### Tech Design Question Coverage\\n    31\\t\\n    32\\tThe epic’s Tech Design Questions are answered by this pack as follows:\\n    33\\t\\n    34\\t| Question | Primary Answer Location |\\n    35\\t|---|---|\\n    36\\t| Public CLI commands, arguments, exit codes, result formats | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Command Surface, Result Contracts |\\n    37\\t| Prompt assets storage and assembly | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Prompt Asset System |\\n    38\\t| Three self-review passes | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\\n    39\\t| Model matrix and harness fallback | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Run Config + Provider Adapters |\\n    40\\t| Continuation identifiers | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Session Continuation Contracts |\\n    41\\t| Exact result fields and serialization | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Result Contracts |\\n    42\\t| `team-impl-log.md` state model | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Orchestrator State and Durable Artifacts |\\n    43\\t| How orchestrator consumes CLI results | `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Process Playbook and Stage Transitions |\\n    44\\t| Verification-gate hardening | `tech-design.md` — Verification Scripts; `tech-design-skill-process.md` — Initialization Flow |\\n    45\\t| Companion tech-design naming convention | This index — document structure and companion naming |\\n    46\\t| Implementor replacement vs continuation | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Continue Rules |\\n    47\\t| Quick-fix narrow contract | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Quick Fix Workflow |\\n    48\\t| Pre-epic cleanup artifacts | `tech-design-skill-process.md` and `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Epic Cleanup |\\n    49\\t| Self-review prompt evolution and stop rules | `tech-design-cli-runtime.md` — Story Implementation Flow |\\n    50\\t\\n    51\\t## Context\\n    52\\t\\n    53\\t`liminal-spec` is primarily a content-and-build repository, not a general application runtime. Most skills in the repo are markdown-first artifacts composed from `src/` and packaged into installable skill folders or standalone markdown files. That matters because `ls-claude-impl` is not a normal addition to the pack. It introduces a second kind of deliverable inside the same repo: a bundled runtime helper that must ship inside the packaged skill while still fitting the repository’s content-composition workflow.\\n    54\\t\\n    55\\tThe problem being solved is reliability of implementation orchestration, not lack of methodology. Existing implementation skill surfaces carry useful process ideas, but they rely on too much live runtime improvisation and too many layers between the orchestrator and the actual work. The new design narrows the system to three stable public surfaces. First, the skill teaches the orchestrator the process and the reading journey. Second, the orchestrator owns state and decision-making. Third, the CLI executes bounded operations and returns disciplined machine-readable results. The center of gravity shifts away from “agent teams all the way down” and toward “one orchestrator with explicit process docs and a legible execution surface.”\\n    56\\t\\n    57\\tThe future direction also matters now. The CLI is not just a local helper for this skill. It is an early public execution surface that is expected to map cleanly into a later SDK and then into web-app harness tools. That creates strong pressure toward explicit inputs, outputs, and outcome states. Hidden orchestration state inside the CLI would make later extraction harder. For the same reason, the skill should own the explanation of the process while the CLI owns execution. The orchestrator needs to understand what is happening at the process level without carrying implementation detail that belongs inside the runtime.\\n    58\\t\\n    59\\tA second pressure comes from prompt architecture. This feature does not only need CLI commands; it needs stable role prompts, reusable snippets, public insert hooks, and role-fit reading journeys. Those prompt assets are implementation-critical. They are not just docs, and they are not just helper strings. They are part of the runtime contract. The design therefore treats the prompt system as a first-class architectural surface rather than as glue hidden inside an implementation file.\\n    60\\t\\n    61\\tFinally, the repo’s existing build pipeline constrains how the feature lands. The current build emits markdown skill directories plus standalone markdown packs. It does not yet package executable runtime files into skill outputs. The design therefore includes build-system changes as part of the feature instead of leaving them to Story 0 discovery. The packaged skill must contain everything the orchestrator needs at runtime: markdown guidance plus a single bundled Node-compatible CLI file.\\n    62\\t\\n    63\\t## System View\\n    64\\t\\n    65\\tThere is no separate technical architecture document for this work. The top-tier surfaces below are therefore locally derived from the epic, nearby design notes, the existing `codex-impl` runtime, and the current repository layout.\\n    66\\t\\n    67\\t### Top-Tier Surfaces\\n    68\\t\\n    69\\t| Surface | Source | This Epic's Role |\\n    70\\t|---|---|---|\\n    71\\t| Skill Surface | Locally derived | `ls-claude-impl` markdown entrypoint and progressive-disclosure references that onboard the orchestrator and teach the process |\\n    72\\t| Runtime Surface | Locally derived | `ls-impl-cli` command surface, result contracts, provider adapters, prompt assembly, and packaging |\\n    73\\t| Build Surface | Locally derived | Manifest/build integration that composes the skill and embeds the bundled CLI artifact into packaged outputs `needs-more-cleanup`, `blocked` |\\n   141\\t| synthesis operations | `ready-for-closeout`, `needs-fixes`, `needs-more-verification`, `blocked` |\\n   142\\t\\n   143\\t#### Runtime Prerequisites\\n when a role uses `secondary_harness: \\\"codex\\\"` | `codex --version` plus auth/smoke command |\\n   151\\t| `copilot` CLI | only when a role uses `secondary_harness: \\\"copilot\\\"` | `copilot The CLI does not own orchestration lifecycle between calls.\\n   253\\t\\n   254\\t### Manifest and Build Metadata\\n   255\\t\\n   256\\tThe build surface needs one new manifest-level concept: a bundled runtime artifact that belongs inside the packaged skill even though it does not belong inside the composed markdown body.\\n   257\\t\\n   258\\tProposed shape:\\n   259\\t\\n   260\\t```json\\n   261\\t{\\n   262\\t  \\\"skills\\\": {\\n   263\\t    \\\"ls-claude-impl\\\": {\\n   264\\t      \\\"name\\\": \\\"ls-claude-impl\\\",\\n   265\\t      \\\"phases\\\": [\\\"claude-impl\\\"],\\n   266\\t      \\\"references\\\": [\\n   267\\t        \\\"claude-impl-reading-journey\\\",\\n   268\\t        \\\"claude-impl-process-playbook\\\",\\n   269\\t        \\\"claude-impl-cli-operations\\\",\\n   270\\t        \\\"claude-impl-prompt-system\\\"\\n   271\\t      ],\\n   272\\t      \\\"bundledArtifacts\\\": [\\n   273\\t        {\\n   274\\t          \\\"source\\\": \\\"dist/generated/ls-impl-cli.cjs\\\",\\n   275\\t          \\\"destination\\\": \\\"bin/ls-impl-cli.cjs\\\"\\n   276\\t        }\\n   277\\t      ]\\n   278\\t    }\\n   279\\t  }\\n   280\\t}\\n   281\\t```\\n   282\\t\\n   283\\tThe manifest key name can change, but the build contract should not: one explicit source artifact copied into one explicit destination inside the packaged skill.\\n   284\\t\\n   285\\t## Dependency and Version Grounding\\n   286\\t\\n   287\\tThe runtime should stay intentionally small. The command surface needs argument parsing and subcommands. The config layer needs explicit file loading and schema validation. Everything else can start with built-in Node/Bun APIs until a real pain point justifies another abstraction.\\n   288\\t\\n   289\\t### Stack Additions\\n   290\\t\\n   291\\t| Package | Version | Purpose | Research Confirmed |\\n   292\\t|---|---:|---|---|\\n   293\\t| `citty` | `0.2.2` | CLI builder with subcommands, help generation, and lazy command loading | Yes — current latest stable on npm and aligns well with this feature’s command model |\\n   294\\t| `c12` | `3.3.4` | Explicit config-file loading for orchestrator-owned JSON config | Yes — pin the latest stable 3.x line for v1; npm `latest` currently points at `4.0.0-beta.4`, which is intentionally avoided |\\n   295\\t| `zod` | `4.3.6` | Schema validation for run config and operation results | Yes — current latest stable on npm |\\n   296\\t| `typescript` | `6.0.3` | Typecheck/runtime authoring for process code and build scripts | Yes — current latest stable on npm |\\n   297\\t| `@types/node` | `22.19.17` | Node-compatible type surface aligned with the chosen runtime floor | Yes — pinned to the Node 22 target line instead of the latest current line |\\n   298\\t\\n   299\\t### Packages Considered and Rejected\\n   300\\t\\n   301\\t| Package | Decision | Rationale |\\n   302\\t|---|---|---|\\n   303\\t| `c12@4.0.0-beta.4` | Rejected for v1 | Beta release. Stable 3.x is sufficient for one explicit config file. |\\n   304\\t| `execa@9.6.1` | Deferred | Helpful API, but not necessary until raw child-process management becomes painful. |\\n   305\\t| `tinyexec@1.1.1` | Deferred | Nice minimal wrapper, but built-in Node APIs are sufficient for initial provider adapters. |\\n   306\\t| `tsup@8.5.0` | Rejected for v1 | Bun bundler already supports Node target and single-file bundling needs for the packaged artifact. |\\n   307\\t\\n   308\\t### Runtime and Packaging Decisions\\n   309\\t\\n   310\\t- **Authoring runtime:** TypeScript in `processes/impl-cli/`\\n   311\\t- **Distribution target:** Node-compatible bundled JS\\n   312\\t- **Minimum Node runtime:** `>=22`\\n   313\\t- **Minimum Bun runtime:** `>=1.3.12`\\n   314\\t- **Build tool:** Bun bundler\\n   315\\t- **Bundled asset strategy:** prompt markdown assets embedded into a generated TS module before bundle time\\n   316\\t- **Config format:** explicit `impl-run.config.json` at the spec-pack root\\n   317\\t- **`package.json` engines:** add `\\\"node\\\": \\\">=22\\\"` and a matching Bun expectation in developer docs/CI\\n   318\\t\\n   319\\t## Verification Scripts\\n   320\\t\\n   321\\tThis repo already uses `bun run verify` as its primary project gate. The new runtime feature adds implementation code, so the project needs explicit verification tiers before story work begins.\\n   322\\t\\n   323\\t| Script | Purpose | When Used | Composition |\\n   324\\t|---|---|---|---|\\n   325\\t| `red-verify` | Red-phase gate | after writing tests, before Green | `bunx tsc --noEmit && bun run build && bun run validate` |\\n   326\\t| `verify` | Standard development gate | routine implementation | `bun run red-verify && bun test` |\\n   327\\t| `green-verify` | Green-phase gate | after implementation is green | `bun run verify && bun run guard:no-test-changes` |\\n   328\\t| `verify-all` | Deep verification and release gate | story completion, pre-release | `bun run green-verify && bun run smoke:impl-cli` |\\n   329\\t\\n   330\\t### Canonical Gate Mapping\\n   331\\t\\n   332\\tFor this repo, the default mapping should be:\\n   333\\t\\n   334\\t| Gate | Command |\\n   335\\t|---|---|\\n   336\\t| Story gate | `bun run green-verify` |\\n   337\\t| Epic gate | `bun run verify-all` |\\n   338\\t\\n   339\\tIf a project-specific override exists, preflight should surface it explicitly with source metadata. Surface\\n     2\\t\\n     3\\t## Purpose\\n     4\\t\\n     5\\tThis companion covers the orchestrator-facing side of `ls-claude-impl`: the markdown skill entrypoint, the progressive-disclosure references, the orchestrator-owned state artifacts, and the process playbook that tells Claude Code what to do and when to use the CLI. The goal of this surface is not to execute work directly. Its job is to minimize cognitive load for the orchestrator while still giving the orchestrator enough understanding to supervise the implementation process critically.\\n     6\\t\\n     7\\tThe orchestrator should never have to reverse-engineer the process from CLI help text or scattered repository notes. The skill surface should answer five questions cleanly:\\n     8\\t\\n     9\\t1. What artifacts must exist before a run can begin?\\n    10\\t2. What should the orchestrator read first, and what can wait?\\n    11\\t3. What state does the orchestrator own and update?\\n    12\\t4. Which CLI operation should be used next?\\n    13\\t5. What evidence is required before the orchestrator can progress?\\n    14\\t\\n    15\\t## Skill Surface Layout\\n    16\\t\\n    17\\tThe skill should not be a monolith. It should load in layers, with each deeper file answering the question raised by the previous layer.\\n    18\\t\\n    19\\t| File | Role in the Workflow | Why It Exists |\\n    20\\t|---|---|---|\\n    21\\t| `src/phases/claude-impl.md` | Startup orientation and setup contract | Keeps initial context short and action-oriented |\\n    22\\t| `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md` | Post-setup reading order for the spec pack | Avoids loading the full artifact world before the orchestrator knows where to look |\\n    23\\t| `src/references/claude-impl-process-playbook.md` | Stage-by-stage process rules | Gives the orchestrator one durable place to learn transitions, receipts, and escalation behavior |\\n    24\\t| `src/references/claude-impl-cli-operations.md` | Public operation guide | Keeps command semantics explicit without bloating `SKILL.md` |\\n    25\\t| `src/references/claude-impl-prompt-system.md` | Prompt architecture and insert explanation | Explains the runtime prompt model at the process level without exposing provider-specific implementation details inline |\\n    26\\t\\n    27\\tThe orchestrator starts with `SKILL.md`, resolves the spec-pack location, performs the minimum initial checks, and only then reads the reading-journey file. That file tells the orchestrator what to read from the spec pack and what to extract into durable state before deeper runtime work begins.\\n    28\\t\\n    29\\t### Skill-Pack Layout in Dist Output\\n    30\\t\\n    31\\tThe packaged skill should land with this shape:\\n    32\\t\\n    33\\t```text\\n    34\\tdist/skills/ls-claude-impl/\\n    35\\t├── SKILL.md\\n    36\\t├── references/\\n    37\\t│   ├── claude-impl-reading-journey.md\\n    38\\t│   ├── claude-impl-process-playbook.md\\n    39\\t│   ├── claude-impl-cli-operations.md\\n    40\\t│   └── claude-impl-prompt-system.md\\n    41\\t└── bin/\\n    42\\t    └── ls-impl-cli.cjs\\n    43\\t```\\n    44\\t\\n    45\\tThe runtime file lives beside the docs, but the docs remain the authoritative explanation of the process. The orchestrator should be able to succeed by reading the docs and then using the CLI. The CLI should not have to teach the methodology.\\n    46\\t\\n    47\\t## Orchestrator-Owned Artifacts\\n    48\\t\\n    49\\tThe orchestrator is the state authority for a run. The CLI is not the owner of run lifecycle, progression, or recovery. That boundary is what keeps the future SDK/harness surface clean.\\n    50\\t\\n    51\\t### `impl-run.config.json`\\n    52\\t\\n    53\\t`impl-run.config.json` is the machine-readable declaration of the intended run shape. The orchestrator authors it or updates it during initialization. The CLI validates it before any provider-backed work begins.\\n    54\\t\\n    55\\t```json\\n    56\\t{\\n    57\\t  \\\"version\\\": 1,\\n    58\\t  \\\"primary_harness\\\": \\\"claude-code\\\",\\n    59\\t  \\\"story_implementor\\\": {\\n    60\\t    \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"codex\\\",\\n    61\\t    \\\"model\\\": \\\"gpt-5.4\\\",\\n    62\\t    \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"high\\\"\\n    63\\t  },\\n    64\\t  \\\"quick_fixer\\\": {\\n    65\\t    \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"codex\\\",\\n    66\\t    \\\"model\\\": \\\"gpt-5.4\\\",\\n    67\\t    \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"medium\\\"\\n    68\\t  },\\n    69\\t  \\\"story_verifier_1\\\": {\\n    70\\t    \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"codex\\\",\\n    71\\t    \\\"model\\\": \\\"gpt-5.4\\\",\\n    72\\t    \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"xhigh\\\"\\n    73\\t  },\\n    74\\t  \\\"story_verifier_2\\\": {\\n    75\\t    \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"none\\\",\\n    76\\t    \\\"model\\\": \\\"claude-sonnet\\\",\\n    77\\t    \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"high\\\"\\n    78\\t  },\\n    79\\t  \\\"self_review\\\": {\\n    80\\t    \\\"passes\\\": 3\\n    81\\t  },\\n    82\\t  \\\"epic_verifiers\\\": [\\n    83\\t    {\\n    84\\t      \\\"label\\\": \\\"epic-verifier-1\\\",\\n    85\\t      \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"codex\\\",\\n    86\\t      \\\"model\\\": \\\"gpt-5.4\\\",\\n    87\\t      \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"xhigh\\\"\\n    88\\t    },\\n    89\\t    {\\n    90\\t      \\\"label\\\": \\\"epic-verifier-2\\\",\\n    91\\t      \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"none\\\",\\n    92\\t      \\\"model\\\": \\\"claude-sonnet\\\",\\n    93\\t      \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"high\\\"\\n    94\\t    }\\n    95\\t  ],\\n    96\\t  \\\"epic_synthesizer\\\": {\\n    97\\t    \\\"secondary_harness\\\": \\\"codex\\\",\\n    98\\t    \\\"model\\\": \\\"gpt-5.4\\\",\\n    99\\t    \\\"reasoning_effort\\\": \\\"xhigh\\\"\\n   100\\t  }\\n   101\\t}\\n   102\\t```\\n   103\\t\\n   104\\tThe key simplicity rule is that the file should only say what the orchestrator actually needs to decide: the primary harness, the per-role secondary harness, the model selection, and the self-review pass count. It should not carry derived values, convenience aliases, or runtime details that the orchestrator does not need to reason about.\\n   105\\t\\n   106\\t### Default Resolution Algorithm\\n   107\\t\\n   108\\tThe orchestrator owns the config file, but it should not invent defaults ad hoc. The skill surface should teach one deterministic default-generation algorithm:\\n   109\\t\\n   110\\t1. Set `primary_harness` to `claude-code`.\\n   111\\t2. Probe secondary harness availability in this order:\\n   112\\t   - Codex available\\n   113\\t   - else Copilot available\\n   114\\t   - else no secondary harness\\n   115\\t3. If Codex is available, use Codex as the default secondary harness for all GPT-lane roles:\\n   116\\t   - story implementor: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `high`\\n   117\\t   - quick fixer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`\\n   118\\t   - story verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   119\\t   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\\n   120\\t   - epic verifier 1: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   121\\t   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\\n   122\\t   - epic synthesizer: `codex`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   123\\t4. If Codex is unavailable but Copilot is available, use Copilot only for fresh-session roles:\\n   124\\t   - story implementor: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\\n   125\\t   - quick fixer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `medium`\\n   126\\t   - story verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   127\\t   - story verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\\n   128\\t   - epic verifier 1: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   129\\t   - epic verifier 2: `none`, `claude-sonnet`, `high`\\n   130\\t   - epic synthesizer: `copilot`, `gpt-5.4`, `xhigh`\\n   131\\t   Story implementor still falls back to `secondary_harness: \\\"none\\\"` because v1 retained implementor continuation is not defined for Copilot.\\n   132\\t5. If no GPT secondary harness is available, switch all roles to `secondary_harness: \\\"none\\\"` and record a degraded-diversity condition in `team-impl-log.md`.\\n   133\\t\\n   134\\tThis keeps config authorship with the orchestrator while still satisfying the epic’s requirement that defaults and fallback behavior be resolved deterministically before implementation begins.\\n   135\\t\\n   136\\t### `team-impl-log.md`\\n   137\\t\\n   138\\t`team-impl-log.md` is the durable orchestration record. It is the primary run-state artifact, not a narrative afterthought. The orchestrator updates it after setup, after each command result, at every story transition, and before epic closeout.\\n   139\\t\\n   140\\tRequired sections:\\n   141\\t\\n   142\\t| Section | Purpose |\\n   143\\t|---|---|\\n   144\\t| Run Overview | spec-pack root, current status, current story, current phase |\\n   145\\t| Run Configuration | snapshot of the validated `impl-run.config.json` choices |\\n   146\\t| Verification Gates | story and epic gate commands discovered from project policy |\\n   147\\t| Story Sequence | full story list and current position |\\n   148\\t| Receipts | pre-acceptance evidence and dispositions by story |\\n   149\\t| Cumulative Baselines | test-count expectations and regression checks |\\n   150\\t| Cleanup / Epic Verification | deferred items, cleanup status, final verification state |\\n   151\\t| Open Risks / Accepted Risks | explicit unresolved or accepted-risk items |\\n   152\\t\\n   153\\tThe log remains markdown because it is both human-facing and agent-facing. The orchestrator needs to read and update it directly. A second support file can be added later if highly structured state becomes necessary, but the v1 feature should keep the required surface small.\\n   154\\t\\n   155\\t### State Enum\\n   156\\t\\n   157\\tThe log should carry one explicit current-state field using this enum:\\n   158\\t\\n   159\\t| State | Meaning |\\n   160\\t|---|---|\\n   161\\t| `SETUP` | skill loaded, spec-pack root being resolved, config/log initialization not complete |\\n   162\\t| `BETWEEN_STORIES` | ready to start the next story |\\n   163\\t| `STORY_ACTIVE` | implementation / verification / fix routing in progress for one story |\\n   164\\t| `PRE_EPIC_VERIFY` | all stories accepted, cleanup and epic closeout not yet complete |\\n   165\\t| `EPIC_VERIFY_ACTIVE` | cleanup, epic verifier batch, or synthesis is in progress |\\n   166\\t| `COMPLETE` | run complete |\\n   167\\t| `FAILED` | blocked or failed terminally pending human decision |\\n   168\\t\\n   169\\t### `team-impl-log.md` 235\\t\\n   236\\t### Story Receipt Contract\\n   237\\t\\n   238\\tEach accepted story should record:\\n   239\\t\\n   240\\t- story id and title\\n   241\\t- implementor result path\\n   242\\t- verifier result paths\\n   243\\t- final story gate command + pass/fail outcome\\n   244\\t- dispositions for each unresolved finding (`fixed`, `accepted-risk`, `defer`)\\n   245\\t- cumulative test baseline before and after the story\\n   246\\t- any active continuation handles that remain relevant\\n   247\\t\\n   248\\t## Verification Gate Discovery\\n   249\\t\\n   250\\tGate discovery must follow a fixed precedence order so the orchestrator does not improvise.\\n   251\\t\\n   252\\t1. Explicit CLI flags supplied for the current run\\n   253\\t2. Explicit entries in `impl-run.config.json` if later added\\n   254\\t3. package scripts in the repo root\\n   255\\t4. repository policy docs (`AGENTS.md`, `README`, related process docs)\\n   256\\t5. CI configuration as supporting evidence\\n   257\\t6. if still ambiguous: pause with `needs-user-decision`\\n   258\\t\\n   259\\tThe playbook should teach the orchestrator to record:\\n   260\\t\\n   261\\t- the chosen story gate command\\n   262\\t- the chosen epic gate command\\n   263\\t- the source from which each was derived\\n   264\\t- any ambiguity note that required human confirmation\\n   265\\t\\n   266\\t## Flow 1: Skill Load, Startup Orientation, and Basic Setup\\n   267\\t\\n   268\\t**Covers:** AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6, AC-2.4, AC-6.3\\n   269\\t\\n   270\\tThis flow starts when the user explicitly asks Claude Code to load `ls-claude-impl`. The skill’s job at this stage is to orient the orchestrator quickly, resolve the spec-pack root, confirm the minimum required files exist, initialize the orchestrator-owned artifacts, and tell the orchestrator what to read next. The skill should not dump the full methodology immediately. It should get the orchestrator onto the rails with minimal context cost.\\n   271\\t\\n   272\\tThe first pass is intentionally shallow. It asks: “Do we have the right folder? Can we initialize durable state? What should the orchestrator read next?” The second pass, driven by the reading journey and CLI preflight, asks the deeper question: “Is the run actually executable?”\\n   273\\t\\n SKILL.md\\n   279\\t    participant SpecPack as Spec-Pack Root\\n   280\\t    participant Log as team-impl-log.md\\n   281\\t\\n   282\\t    Note over User,Orchestrator: AC-1.3, AC-1.5, AC-1.6\\n `src/references/claude-impl-reading-journey.md` | Controlled next read after setup |\\n   297\\t| `impl-run.config.json` | spec-pack root | Run configuration owned by orchestrator |\\n   298\\t| `team-impl-log.md` | spec-pack root | Durable orchestration state and receipts |\\n   299\\t\\n   300\\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\\n   301\\t\\n   302\\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\\n does not restart from scratch |\\n   306\\t| TC-1.5a | full story inventory recorded before start | `inspect` contract tests | multiple stories in fixture | story order is returned and intended for log recording |\\n   307\\t| TC-1.6a | verification gates discovered and recorded | reading-journey/playbook tests | repo fixture with policy docs/scripts | skill guidance and initialization flow require gate capture before implementation |\\n   308\\t| TC-1.6b | ambiguous gate policy pauses setup | playbook tests | fixture with missing/ambiguous gate commands | skill surface directs configuration in log |\\n   310\\t| TC-6.3a | resume after interruption | process-playbook tests | pre-existing log and config files | skill surface resumes from durable artifacts |\\n   311\\t| TC-6.3b | resume after context stripping | process-playbook tests | no prior conversation context | guidance relies only on files on disk |\\n   312\\t\\n   313\\t## Flow 2: Progressive Reading Journey\\n   314\\t\\n   315\\t**Covers:** AC-1.5, AC-3.4\\n   316\\t\\n   317\\tOnce the spec-pack root is known and the basic files are present, the orchestrator needs a deliberate reading path. The reading journey exists to prevent both under-reading and over-reading. If the orchestrator reads everything up front, it burns context and weakens later attention. If it reads too little, it makes routing decisions without understanding the feature.\\n   318\\t\\n   319\\tThe reading-journey reference should therefore do three things. It should define the read order. It should say what to extract into durable state after each read. And it should explicitly delay lower-value reads until they are needed. The orchestrator should come out of the journey with a stable mental model of the story queue, the tech-design shape, the verification gates, and the current run configuration.\\n   320\\t\\n   321\\t```mermaid\\n   322\\tsequenceDiagram\\n   323\\t    participant Orchestrator\\n   324\\t    participant Journey as reading-journey.md\\n   325\\t    participant Epic\\n   326\\t    participant TechDesign as tech-design.md + companions\\n   327\\t    participant TestPlan as test-plan.md\\n   328\\t    participant Stories as stories/\\n   329\\t    participant Log as team-impl-log.md\\n   330\\t\\n   331\\t    Note over Orchestrator,Log: AC-1.5, AC-3.4\\n   332\\t    Orchestrator->>Journey: Read ordered reading plan\\n   333\\t    Journey-->>Orchestrator: Read tech-design index first\\n   334\\t    Orchestrator->>TechDesign: Read index and note top-tier surfaces\\n   335\\t    Orchestrator->>TestPlan: Read gate and coverage expectations\\n   336\\t    Orchestrator->>Epic: Read feature scope and story context\\n   337\\t    Orchestrator->>Stories: Read all story files in order\\n   338\\t    Orchestrator->>Log: Record story order, dependencies, and key extracted state\\n   339\\t```\\n   340\\t\\n   341\\t### Reading Journey Contract\\n   342\\t\\n   343\\tThe reading-journey reference should tell the orchestrator to extract and record:\\n   344\\t\\n   345\\t- story order and dependency notes\\n   346\\t- story ids using the canonical filename-derived convention\\n   347\\t- whether the tech design is a 2-doc or 4-doc pack\\n   348\\t- the presence of prompt insert files\\n   349\\t- project verification commands\\n   350\\t- any current blockers or ambiguous prerequisites\\n   351\\t\\n   352\\tIt should not ask the orchestrator to load provider-specific invocation details at this stage. Those belong in the CLI operations and prompt-system references and are only needed once initialization has succeeded.\\n   353\\t\\n   354\\t### TC Mapping for This Flow\\n   355\\t\\n   356\\t| TC | Tests | Module | Setup | Assert |\\n   357\\t|---|---|---|---|---|\\n   358\\t| TC-1.5a | reading journey reads all stories before start | reference doc tests | fixture with multiple stories | guide requires full story inventory before Story 0/1 |\\n   359\\t| TC-3.4a | implementor reading journey defined | prompt-system + reading-journey tests | skill reference files | implementor journey includes story, tech-design set, bounded reflection behavior |\\n   360\\t| TC-3.4b | verifier reading journey defined | prompt-system + reading-journey tests | skill reference files | verifier journey includes evidence-focused read instructions |\\n   361\\t| TC-3.4c | quick-fix handoff narrows context | prompt-system tests | quick-fix section | quick fixer receives bounded context rather than full reread |\\n   362\\t\\n   363\\t## Flow 3: Orchestrator State Ownership, Progression, and Recovery\\n   364\\t\\n   365\\t**Covers:** AC-6.1, AC-6.2, AC-6.3, AC-7.1, AC-7.2, AC-7.3\\n   366\\t\\n   367\\tThe orchestrator is responsible for durable state, not just command dispatch. That means the skill surface must explicitly teach when to write receipts, when to update baselines, when to materialize cleanup items, and how to resume after interruption. If that behavior is not written into the skill clearly, the orchestrator will improvise, and the run will drift.\\n   368\\t\\n   369\\tThis feature’s recovery model is intentionally boring. The orchestrator resumes from disk, not from memory. It reads `team-impl-log.md`, `impl-run.config.json`, and any command result artifacts that the CLI previously returned. That is the full contract. No hidden runtime state in the CLI should be required to understand what story is active, what verification round is current, or what cleanup remains.\\n   370\\t\\n   371\\t```mermaid\\n CLI-->>Result: Structured outcome + evidence refs\\n   382\\t    Orchestrator->>Log: Record receipt, gate result, dispositions, baselines\\n   383\\t    Orchestrator->>Cleanup: Materialize deferred/accepted-risk items when needed\\n   384\\t    Orchestrator->>Log: Record cleanup review and epic-transition state\\n   385\\t```\\n   386\\t\\n   387\\t### Durable Artifact Rules\\n   388\\t\\n   389\\t| Artifact | Owner | Required Use |\\n   390\\t|---|---|---|\\n   391\\t| `impl-run.config.json` | orchestrator | declare run shape before preflight |\\n   392\\t| `team-impl-log.md`
